  result, and the closed-form s-wave interpolation is stated at zero Zeeman field SumRule-v1.tex:50.

  because it had g_z=0 and could not produce \Pi_{xz}\neq 0 as written SumRule-v1.tex:286.

  F_s(5)=0.909; half-saturation is \lambda \approx 1.317 SumRule-v1.tex:529, check_sumrule.wl:21.
  - The multiband inter-band-mixing paragraph and the triplet-extension paragraph are now marked as baseline/heuristic
  rather than exact derived statements SumRule-v1.tex:397, SumRule-v1.tex:566.

  Build note: a fresh BibTeX run for SumRule-v1.tex fails because mag-soc.bib already contains a duplicated pang2025
  entry. I therefore compiled the revised note using the existing bibliography output copied from SumRule.bbl.

вҖә Numerical cross-check of F_s(\lambda)? Evaluate F_s(g K_F/\Delat) and overlay it with Fig.3 of the published PRB (s-
  wave ПҮ(T=0)/ПҮ_N vs gk_F at fixed T=0). If the curves match, this is a strong independent validation of the closed-
  form derivation. If they don't, the algebra in Sec. VI.B needs revisiting before anything is circulated.

  T=0 s-wave curve from the local PDF, evaluate the closed form on the same axis, and check whether the overlay agrees
  within digitization error.

  1. Parse the local PRB Fig. 3 vector graphic and extract the T=0 intercepts for the five published gk_F/k_B T_{c0}
  curves.

  3. Export a reproducible overlay plot and a comparison table.
  4. Decide from the actual mismatch level whether Sec. VI.B is validated or needs rework.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

  packaging that into reproducible scripts plus a plot/table so you can inspect the comparison directly rather than
  trust a one-off calculation.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

  ПҮ_N, with RMS about 7.7Г—10^-4. IвҖҷm still generating the plot artifacts so you have the visual overlay and the raw
  table on disk.

вҖў The plot artifacts are ready. IвҖҷm checking the exported overlay image itself now to make sure the labels and residual
  panel are readable before I conclude from it.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў IвҖҷm tightening the script dependency now so it no longer relies on a temporary file in /tmp; it will regenerate the
  page-5 SVG from the local PRB PDF itself. That makes the validation rerunnable from the repo.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў Fig. 3 is a family of \chi(T)/\chi_N curves vs T/T_{c0}, so the relevant test of F_s is its T=0 cross-section: the
  left-endpoint values for the published gk_F/(k_B T_{c0}) = 0,1,2,4,30 curves. I extracted those endpoints directly
  from the page-5 vector graphic and compared them to
  (2/3) F_s(\lambda) with
  \lambda = [gk_F/(k_B T_{c0})] / [\Delta_0/(k_B T_{c0})]
  and \Delta_0/(k_B T_{c0}) = \pi/e^{\gamma_E} = 1.763876988862046.

  The match is excellent. The maximum absolute mismatch is 1.50e-3 in \chi/\chi_N, and the RMS mismatch is 7.69e-4. The
  largest residual is at gk_F/(k_B T_{c0})=30, where the digitized point is 0.66005 and the closed form gives 0.65855.
  At 1,2,4, the differences are 2.69e-4, 1.59e-4, and 5.08e-4. That is fully consistent with line-thickness / plotting-
  resolution level differences, not an algebraic failure.

  This is strong independent validation of the zero-field s-wave closed form in Sec. VI.B. Based on this check, I do not
  see evidence that the algebra in Sec. VI.B needs revisiting before circulation.

  Artifacts:

  - Comparison table: fig3_t0_comparison.tsv
  - Overlay plot: fig3_t0_validation.png, fig3_t0_validation.pdf
  - Summary: fig3_t0_summary.txt
  - Reproducible scripts: validate_fig3_t0.py, validate_fig3_t0.wl

  If you want, I can now fold this check into the note as a short appendix or a one-paragraph validation remark.

  spherical-FS relation \chi(0)/\chi_N=(2/3)F_s(\lambda) is already stated. Next IвҖҷm checking the figure style and

  reproducible path is direct digitization plus a numerical script, or whether an existing self-consistency pipeline is
  already hidden in the workspace.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The page-6 SVG export succeeded, but pdftocairo named it with its usual suffix rather than the exact filename I
  passed. IвҖҷm locating that export and then parsing the vector paths for Fig. 5(a) the same way I did for Fig. 3, except
  this time we need full curves and kink locations, not just left endpoints.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The SVG confirms the same five colored curve families used in Fig. 3, which is a good sign. I still need the plot box
  for panel (a) and the marker geometry for the BFS kinks, so IвҖҷm rasterizing page 6 once for visual inspection before
  writing the extraction script.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў I have the right source page now. The finite-field panel is the lower-left plot on page 6, so the next step is
  geometric: isolate that plot box in the SVG, enumerate the colored curve path extents, and separate the filled kink
  markers from the curve traces before I write the numerical validation script.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў I found a substantive inconsistency before writing any code: the actual local PRB page-6 Fig. 5(a) shows four fixed-
  SOC curves labeled gk_F/(k_B T_c0)=1,3,5,10, not the five-curve set {0,0.5,1,2,4} described in Validation.tex and
  SumRule-v2.tex. IвҖҷm checking the paper text around the finite-field section now so I can decide whether to build the
  validation against the actual published panel or whether there is a second source figure/page you intended.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The paper text confirms the qualitative finite-field story but not the legend values in plain text; the page image
  remains the authoritative source here, and it shows the four-curve g={1,3,5,10} panel. IвҖҷm reading the gap/self-
  consistency equations next so I can compute the overlay rather than just digitize the published lines.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The paper gives the self-consistency loop in the standard BdG form but not a canned numeric routine, so IвҖҷll need to
  implement it. Before I do that, IвҖҷm extracting the exact page-6 plot geometry and marker locations; that will let me
  compare any computed curve against the published data immediately and catch model/convention mistakes early.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў IвҖҷm switching to the actual finite-field Hamiltonian now. The fastest way to know whether this is tractable is to let
  Wolfram diagonalize the 4x4 BdG matrix symbolically for one (\xi,\theta) slice and see whether the eigenvalues
  simplify to something usable for the self-consistency and susceptibility integrals.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў I have the panel geometry and marker positions. IвҖҷm pressure-testing the expensive part now: one actual self-
  consistency integral for the finite-field BdG problem. If that converges in reasonable time, IвҖҷll turn it into the
  reproducible fig5 pipeline; if not, IвҖҷll simplify the integrals before writing files.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў I have enough to build the missing asset now. One more practical check first: IвҖҷm scanning SumRule-v2.tex for every
  \includegraphics call so I donвҖҷt stop at fig5 if the revised manuscript still has other unresolved figure
  dependencies.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў I have the missing figure pipeline scoped. IвҖҷm adding two reproducible scripts now: one to extract the actual page-6
  Fig. 5(a) curve data from the vector graphic, and one to solve the finite-field gap equation plus the Validation.tex
  closed-form overlay and export figures/fig5_finiteH_validation.pdf.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The first draft of the pipeline is in place. IвҖҷm doing a quick code audit before running it, because there are a
  couple of Wolfram-language details that are easy to get subtly wrong here: association construction, legend
  attachment, and filtering out any Indeterminate theory points before plotting.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў I found the usual WL gotchas: one association was being built incorrectly, and the legend attachment was too
  optimistic for a Show[...] object. IвҖҷm tightening those before the first full run so the figure generation doesnвҖҷt
  fail two minutes in on a trivial syntax issue.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The digitization step is clean. The extracted panel confirms actual local values g={1,3,5,10} with three BFS markers
  at Hc2/H0вүҲ1.209,1.269,1.340. IвҖҷm running the Wolfram pipeline now to solve the gap equation point-by-point and export
  the overlay PDF.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The Wolfram run is still in the numerical part, which is expected here because it is solving the self-consistent gap
  branch curve-by-curve and then doing a second set of susceptibility integrals on top. IвҖҷm letting it finish rather
  than relaxing the settings prematurely, because the first-pass timings suggested the full run is feasible without
  cutting corners.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў IвҖҷm checking the filesystem while the long Wolfram job runs so I can tell whether it is still making progress or
  whether it got stuck in a bad numerical branch. If there are no partial outputs after this interval, IвҖҷll cut the cost
  by thinning the H grid or simplifying the legend/render stage rather than waiting blindly.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The first full run is too expensive because it evaluates the gap equation at every raw SVG point. IвҖҷm thinning that to
  a regular field grid per curve and interpolating back onto the digitized points for the residuals, which preserves the
  figure content but cuts the runtime by a large factor.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The first Wolfram job would keep running with the heavier point set and could overwrite the lighter rerun if I leave
  it alone. IвҖҷm stopping that older validation job and rerunning the optimized version on a regular field grid so the
  outputs are deterministic.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The optimized run is still slower than the single-point timings suggested, but it has not obviously stalled yet. IвҖҷm
  giving it one more interval before I cut the grid further; the next decision point is whether a 15вҖ“20 point field grid
  is enough for the overlay if this pass still hasnвҖҷt produced the PDF.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The bottleneck is the nested numerical integration around the kink region, not the SVG side. IвҖҷm cutting the field
  grid and the quadrature goals one more notch and adding curve-by-curve progress prints so the rerun is both faster and
  observable.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The lighter run is healthy now. It has finished the g=1 curve, so the runtime issue was indeed point-count driven
  rather than a bad symbolic path. IвҖҷm letting the remaining three curves finish and then IвҖҷll verify the exported PDF
  and rebuild SumRule-v2.tex.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The numeric output is usable, but the first render exposed a plotting bug: the curve colors were being looked up with
  numeric keys that didnвҖҷt match the parsed g values exactly, so everything fell back to Mathematica defaults. IвҖҷm
  fixing the color mapping and regenerating the figure from the saved comparison table rather than recomputing the heavy
  integrals.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The replot one-liner ran into quoting issues, which is not worth debugging in shell form. IвҖҷm adding a tiny standalone
  WL replot script instead; it will read the saved TSV outputs and only handle rendering, so itвҖҷs fast and reusable.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The data are now rendered correctly. IвҖҷm nudging the legend lower-right so it stops sitting on top of the green and
  blue high-field data; then IвҖҷll rebuild SumRule-v2.tex against the finished PDF asset.

вҖў Context compacted

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў Using local-calculation-plotting because this is a local WL/Python validation and figure-generation task. IвҖҷm checking
  the generated finite-field artifacts and the relevant manuscript/validation text before deciding whether anything
  still needs to be rerun or cleaned up.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў The finite-field validation artifacts are present. The important result is that the generated comparison does not
  support the current manuscript claim: the local published panel and the formula in Validation.tex disagree strongly,
  and the parameter set in the note/manuscript does not match the actual page-6 figure.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў I have the scripts and current comparison summary in hand. Next IвҖҷm checking the rendered panel itself and then
  deciding whether to do a light layout cleanup or leave the asset as-is and just report the scientific outcome.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў IвҖҷm making one layout-only patch before rerendering: move the legend out of the data region and typeset its label
  properly. IвҖҷm not changing the numerics; this is just to make the validation figure cleaner to inspect.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў Validation.tex:1 is patched. It now matches the actual local Fig. 5(a) source: the curve set is gk_F/(k_B T_{c0}) =
  1,3,5,10 instead of {0,0.5,1,2,4} (Validation.tex:25), and the note records the observed failed validation with the
  measured residuals max = 0.930059, RMS = 0.265642 (Validation.tex:158).

  I also made the note standalone-cleaner by importing labels from SumRule-v2.tex:1 via xr-hyper and resolving pang2026
  from mag-soc.bib (Validation.tex:2, Validation.tex:248). latexmk -pdf -interaction=nonstopmode -halt-on-error
  Validation.tex now succeeds and writes Validation.pdf.

  The generated figure remains figures/fig5_finiteH_validation.pdf.

  in the original derivation and checking whether the claimed non-spherical-FS formula is actually consistent with the
  2D and quasi-1D examples in the manuscript.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

  about finite-field factorization and inter-band pairing are actually supported by the manuscriptвҖҷs own assumptions,
  not just syntactically correct.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

  already proven for generic inter-band multi-orbital pairing. IвҖҷm tracing the BdG assumptions in the earlier sections
  so I can tighten that claim instead of leaving a loophole in the logic.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

  -> v6 edits now to make sure there isnвҖҷt a third equation-level regression hiding later in the manuscript.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў SumRule-v7.tex is ready, and it compiles to SumRule-v7.pdf. The Wolfram audit is saved as check_sumrule_v6.wl.

  I corrected three substantive issues from v6:

  - removed the unsupported non-spherical trace-bound formula and replaced it with the exact strong-locking trace
  statement Tr ПҮ(0)=2ПҮ_N for arbitrary FS texture in that regime
  - fixed the finite-field discussion: the simple 2x2 sector factorization is the collinear case H || g, while H вҹӮ g /
  equal |b_k| alone does not block-diagonalize the BdG problem
  - tightened the K$_2$Cr$_3$As$_3}$ mechanism claims so the common-\mathbf d interpretation is clearly labeled as the
  orbital-diagonal baseline inference, with inter-band pairing left open

  wolframscript -file check_sumrule_v6.wl now returns clean True checks on the audited identities, and latexmk -pdf
  -interaction=nonstopmode -halt-on-error SumRule-v7.tex succeeds. If you want, I can also generate a marked v6 -> v7
  diff.


вҖә pls generate a marked diff


вҖў IвҖҷm generating a v6 -> v7 marked LaTeX diff now and then compiling it to make sure the red-marked output is usable,
  not just syntactically produced.

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

вҖў latexdiff produced the marked source with the usual parser warnings around REVTeX/BibTeX boilerplate. IвҖҷm compiling

  - SumRule-v8.tex:722: Protocol A now says you can use \sum r_\alpha \le 2 via Eq. (Khf-trace) as a вҖңgeneral FSвҖ
    fallback. But SumRule-v8.tex:669 defines Eq. (Khf-trace) for principal-axis ratios in the strong-locking, diagonal-
    coaxial hyperfine setting, not for arbitrary fixed-axis r_\alpha. The protocol table at SumRule-v8.tex:703 still
    lists Protocol AвҖҷs assumption as вҖң3D isotropic FS.вҖ0;в ј X-Sum-Rule
0;в ҙ X-Sum-Rule
0;в Ұ X-Sum-Rule
0;в § X-Sum-Rule

  - SumRule-v8.tex:526 and SumRule-v8.tex:801 now say the site bound / multiband trace inequality remain valid
    вҖңregardlessвҖқ of inter-band or orbitally mixed pairing, but SumRule-v8.tex:213 still says trace-bound violation can
    signal multi-orbital physics. Those scope statements conflict. The manuscript needs one consistent position here.0;в Ү X-Sum-Rule
0;в Ҹ X-Sum-Rule

  - SumRule-v8.tex:40 and SumRule-v8.tex:821 again state the K$_2$Cr$_3$As$_3$ data select a rigid common \mathbf d
    \parallel \hat c / OSP interpretation, while SumRule-v8.tex:860 correctly weakens that to a baseline inference
    within the orbital-diagonal model. Those two conclusion levels should not coexist.

  SumRule-v8.tex does compile, and the reused Wolfram audit check_sumrule_v6.wl still returns clean True checks on the
  already-audited identities. So the issues I found are logical/scope inconsistencies, not a fresh algebra failure in
  the formulas covered by that script.

  If you want, I can patch these into v9.0;в Ӣ X-Sum-Rule
0;X-Sum-Rule

вҖў Edited SumRule-v9.tex (+11 -11)
     39  	\begin{abstract}
     40 -		We present a unified sum-rule framework for the NMR response of single-band noncentrosymmetric supercon
         ductors in arbitrary Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC), organized in two parts. \emph{Part
         ~I} (theory) establishes the framework. A Plancherel identity $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+\sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr
         }_{s}(O^{2})$, valid for every Hermitian single-particle operator $O$, underlies (i) a trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}
         \,\chi(0)\le\tfrac{2}{3}\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T_{c})=2\chi_{N}$ on a 3D isotropic Fermi surface; (ii) a strong-lock
         ing anisotropic identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$ with the locking projector $
         \Pi_{\mu\nu}=\langle\hat n_{\mu}\hat n_{\nu}\rangle_{\rm FS}$; (iii) a closed-form $s$-wave interpolation $F_{s
         }(\lambda)=1-\sinh^{-1}\lambda/[\lambda\sqrt{1+\lambda^{2}}]$ at arbitrary SOC, validated against existing nume
         rics to $10^{-3}$; (iv) a finite-field extension $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0,\mathbf{H})=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu
         \nu}(\mathbf{H})]$ stable to $O(h^{2}/\lambda^{2})$ against small applied fields; (v) a spin analogue of the Fe
         rrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule tying the Knight-shift deficit to integrated pair-breaking spectral weight; and
          (vi) the multi-band decomposition. \emph{Part~II} (manual) converts these identities into experimental practic
         e: the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem and the two-dimensional simplex of pairing classes (one-to-one map from e
         llipsoid geometry to locking texture); a hyperfine-invariant site-resolved bound as an internal consistency che
         ck; and six protocols covering trace-bound tests, full-ellipsoid diagnostics, finite-field SOC-texture spectros
         copy, $1/T_{1}$ cross-checks, site-resolved tests, and pocket decomposition. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data
         on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, the framework places the observed projection at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$,
         saturates the trace bound, selects a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ on all three bands over the indep
         endent-pocket SOC-texture baseline, and identifies the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ as a fingerprint
          of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation gap formation. Regimes outside strong locking, most nota
         bly Bogoliubov-Fermi-surface formation, are explicitly outside the closed-form reach of the framework.
     40 +		We present a unified sum-rule framework for the NMR response of single-band noncentrosymmetric supercon
         ductors in arbitrary Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC), organized in two parts. \emph{Part
         ~I} (theory) establishes the framework. A Plancherel identity $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+\sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr
         }_{s}(O^{2})$, valid for every Hermitian single-particle operator $O$, underlies (i) a trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}
         \,\chi(0)\le\tfrac{2}{3}\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T_{c})=2\chi_{N}$ on a 3D isotropic Fermi surface; (ii) a strong-lock
         ing anisotropic identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$ with the locking projector $
         \Pi_{\mu\nu}=\langle\hat n_{\mu}\hat n_{\nu}\rangle_{\rm FS}$; (iii) a closed-form $s$-wave interpolation $F_{s
         }(\lambda)=1-\sinh^{-1}\lambda/[\lambda\sqrt{1+\lambda^{2}}]$ at arbitrary SOC, validated against existing nume
         rics to $10^{-3}$; (iv) a finite-field extension $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0,\mathbf{H})=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu
         \nu}(\mathbf{H})]$ stable to $O(h^{2}/\lambda^{2})$ against small applied fields; (v) a spin analogue of the Fe
         rrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule tying the Knight-shift deficit to integrated pair-breaking spectral weight; and
          (vi) the multi-band decomposition. \emph{Part~II} (manual) converts these identities into experimental practic
         e: the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem and the two-dimensional simplex of pairing classes (one-to-one map from e
         llipsoid geometry to locking texture); a hyperfine-invariant site-resolved bound as an internal consistency che
         ck; and six protocols covering trace-bound tests, full-ellipsoid diagnostics, finite-field SOC-texture spectros
         copy, $1/T_{1}$ cross-checks, site-resolved tests, and pocket decomposition. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data
         on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, the framework places the observed projection at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$,
         saturates the trace bound, and, within the orbital-diagonal baseline, favors a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\paralle
         l\hat c$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture one, while identifying the suppression of $1/T_
         {1}\parallel\hat c$ as a fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation gap formation. Regim
         es outside strong locking, most notably Bogoliubov-Fermi-surface formation, are explicitly outside the closed-f
         orm reach of the framework.
     41  	\end{abstract}
        вӢ®
    525  	
    526 -	Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} neglects inter-band pairing (exact for orbital-diagonal pairing). For orbi
         tally mixed pairing, the formula is a decoupled-pocket baseline; deviations diagnose the inter-band structure.
         The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of the orbital-diagonal assumptio
         n; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbita
         l derivation is required before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II to genuine inter-band pairing;
         the experimental bound Eq.~\eqref{eq:site-bound} and the trace inequality Eq.~\eqref{eq:multiband-bound} remain
          valid regardless.
    526 +		Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} neglects inter-band pairing (exact for orbital-diagonal pairing). For
         orbitally mixed pairing, the formula is a decoupled-pocket baseline; deviations diagnose the inter-band structu
         re. The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of the orbital-diagonal assum
         ption; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-or
         bital derivation is therefore required before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalize
         d site/pocket bounds used there, to genuine inter-band pairing.

         exture prediction Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} disagrees with the observation by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knigh
         t-shift units, selecting a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector mechanism within the orbital-diagonal baseline; (c) the o
         bserved suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT budget and diagnos

         ipsoid}. Reading against Table~\ref{tab:ellipsoids}, Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi} is the signature of an \emph{OS
         P unitary spin-triplet with $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$}, or equivalently a quasi-1D SOC locking vector $\hat n
         \parallel\hat c$ on every pocket. This reproduces---via the ellipsoid diagnostic of Protocol~B, without invokin
         g an $\mathbf{H}$-$T$ phase diagram---the principal conclusion of Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021}.
    821 +		The observed point sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} within experimen
         tal uncertainty, and $\sum\pi_{i}=1\pm O(\epsilon)$ saturates the universal constraint Eq.~\eqref{eq:trace-from
         -ellipsoid}. At the level of the measured ellipsoid, Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi} fixes the effective locking tex
         ture to the $\hat c$ vertex of the simplex. Within the canonical single-band catalogue of Table~\ref{tab:ellips
         oids}, this matches the \emph{OSP unitary spin-triplet with $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$} limit, or equivalently
          the quasi-1D $\hat n\parallel\hat c$ vertex. This reproduces---via the ellipsoid diagnostic of Protocol~B, wit
         hout invoking an $\mathbf{H}$-$T$ phase diagram---the principal conclusion of Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} at the le
         vel of the strong-locking single-band framework.

    860 -	We distinguish two levels of conclusion. \emph{Robust experimental statement}: the Knight-shift tensor sits
          at the $\hat c$ vertex, and any acceptable microscopic theory must reproduce that near-vertex ellipsoid. \emph
         {Baseline theoretical inference}: within the orbital-diagonal framework of Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred}, the da
         ta favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline. A defini
         tive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter-band pairing requires a multi-orbital BdG analysis--
         -the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} survives that extension unchanged; only the specific a
         nisotropic identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} needs refinement.

         sits at the $\hat c$ vertex, and any acceptable microscopic theory must reproduce that near-vertex ellipsoid. \
         emph{Baseline theoretical inference}: within the orbital-diagonal framework of Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred}, th
         e data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline. A de
         finitive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter-band pairing requires a multi-orbital BdG analys
         is---the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} survives that extension unchanged, but the specifi
         c anisotropic identities and the pocket/site bounds used in Part~II require rederivation.

    871 -	Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} walked the protocols through the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Trip
         let2021} on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ sits at the oblate-axial
         $\hat c$ vertex of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} and saturates the trace bound. Combined with the band-resolved DOS fr
         actions reported there, the decoupled-pocket structural prediction of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband-A2Cr3As3} misses
         the observed vertex by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift units; within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the d
         ata favor a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ on all three bands over the independent-pocket SOC-texture
          mechanism. The observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot originate from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-
         FGT budget and is a direct fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation, consistent wit
         h the Curie-Weiss enhancement of $1/T_{1}T$ above $T_{c}$.
    871 +		Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} walked the protocols through the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{
         Triplet2021} on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ sits at the oblate-ax
         ial $\hat c$ vertex of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} and saturates the trace bound. Combined with the band-resolved DO
         S fractions reported there, the decoupled-pocket structural prediction of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband-A2Cr3As3} mis
         ses the observed vertex by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift units; within the orbital-diagonal baseline, t
         he data favor a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture
          mechanism. The observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot originate from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-
         FGT budget and is a direct fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation, consistent wit
         h the Curie-Weiss enhancement of $1/T_{1}T$ above $T_{c}$.
    872  	
    873 -	The framework unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric principle: total matrix-element w
         eight is the trace-squared of the probe operator in the single-particle basis. Combined with band-structure inp
         ut, it constrains different spin-triplet mechanisms at the same observed pairing symmetry. It invites systemati
         c tests across the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family under pressure and composition tuning and across different NM
         R-active sites within a single compound. A definitive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter-ban
         d pairing requires a multi-orbital extension of the specific anisotropic identity; the underlying Plancherel id
         entity survives that extension unchanged.
    873 +		The framework unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric principle: total matrix-eleme

         matic tests across the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family under pressure and composition tuning and across differen
         t NMR-active sites within a single compound. A definitive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter
         -band pairing requires a multi-orbital extension of the specific anisotropic identities and protocol-level pock
         et/site bounds; the underlying Plancherel identity survives that extension unchanged.

вҖў Added latexdiff-red-preamble.tex (+17 -0)
     1 +\RequirePackage[normalem]{ulem}
     2 +\RequirePackage{color}
     3 +\definecolor{DIFRED}{rgb}{1,0,0}
     4 +\providecommand{\DIFadd}[1]{{\protect\color{DIFRED}\uwave{#1}}}
     5 +\providecommand{\DIFdel}[1]{{\protect\color{DIFRED}\sout{#1}}}
     6 +\providecommand{\DIFaddbegin}{}
     7 +\providecommand{\DIFaddend}{}
     8 +\providecommand{\DIFdelbegin}{}
     9 +\providecommand{\DIFdelend}{}
    10 +\providecommand{\DIFmodbegin}{}
    11 +\providecommand{\DIFmodend}{}
    12 +\providecommand{\DIFaddFL}[1]{\DIFadd{#1}}
    13 +\providecommand{\DIFdelFL}[1]{\DIFdel{#1}}
    14 +\providecommand{\DIFaddbeginFL}{}

0;в Ҹ X-Sum-Rule

  The substantive fixes in v9 are:

  - Protocol A is now correctly limited to the 3D isotropic-FS trace bound, with general cases redirected to Protocol B
    at SumRule-v9.tex:722.
  - The multiband/inter-band caveat now consistently says the Part II simplex and normalized site/pocket bounds need
    rederivation in a genuine multi-orbital setting at SumRule-v9.tex:526 and SumRule-v9.tex:801.
  - The K(_2)Cr(_3)As(_3) interpretation is softened to вҖңrobust ellipsoid statement + baseline theoretical inference,вҖқ
    rather than an unconditional microscopic selection, at SumRule-v9.tex:806, SumRule-v9.tex:821, and SumRule-
    v9.tex:860.

  I also reran the existing Wolfram audit with wolframscript -file check_sumrule_v6.wl. All audited identities returned
  True, including the F_s(\lambda) checks, ellipsoid algebra, and finite-field relations. The only Wolfram output was
  the same benign NIntegrate::precw / Limit::alimv warnings already associated with the small-\lambda and limit checks;
  no algebraic failures appeared.0;X-Sum-Rule

вҖў Edited SumRule-v10.tex (+26 -20)
    323  	$\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)/\chi_{N}=2\langle F_{s}(\lambda_{\mathbf{k}})\rangle_{\rm FS}\le 2$, strict for finit
         e SOC. For uniform SOC the relation is invertible: $\lambda=F_{s}^{-1}(\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)/2\chi_{N})$, yieldi
         ng a pair-breaking spectroscopy of $|\mathbf{g}|/\Delta$ from three-axis Knight shift (deployed as Protocol~B i
         n Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols}).
    324 -	
    325 -	\subsection{Numerical cross-check against published Fig.~3}
    326 -	
    327 -	Extracting the $T=0$ intercepts of the five $s$-wave curves in Fig.~3 of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}, labeled by $
         gk_{F}/(k_{B}T_{c0})=0,1,2,4,30$, and converting via BCS $\Delta_{0}/(k_{B}T_{c0})=\pi/e^{\gamma_{E}}=1.7638769
         \ldots$,
    328 -	\begin{equation}
    329 -		\chi(0)/\chi_{N}=\tfrac{2}{3}F_{s}(gk_{F}/(k_{B}T_{c0})\cdot e^{\gamma_{E}}/\pi),
    330 -		\label{eq:fig3-crosscheck}
    331 -	\end{equation}
    332 -	yields the overlay in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig3-t0-validation}: max deviation $1.5\times 10^{-3}$ in $\chi/\chi_{N
         }$, RMS $7.7\times 10^{-4}$.
    333 -	
    334 -	\begin{figure}[t]
    335 -		\centering
    336 -		\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/fig3_t0_validation}
    337 -		\caption{Cross-check of Eq.~\eqref{eq:Fs} against the $T=0$ endpoints of the $s$-wave curves in Fig.~3
         of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}. Solid line: Eq.~\eqref{eq:fig3-crosscheck}. Symbols: extracted intercepts. Lower panel
         : digitized minus theory.}
    338 -		\label{fig:fig3-t0-validation}
    339 -	\end{figure}
    340 -	
    341 -	\subsection{Heuristic extension to unitary triplet with anisotropic gap}
    342 -	
    343 -	Replacing $\Delta\to|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|$ in the $\xi$-integration suggests the ansatz $F_{\rm t}(\lamb
         da_{\mathbf{k}})\approx F_{s}(|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|/|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|)$, with $F_{\rm t}\to 1$ at no
         des. This captures the gap-magnitude dependence exactly when the pairing amplitude is spatially constant across
          the FS, but misses corrections from the $\hat d$-vector angular structure and from intra-band triplet channels
         . A rigorous derivation is beyond the present scope.
    324 +	An independent numerical overlay against the published BdG curves of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}, together with a
         brief heuristic note on anisotropic-gap unitary triplets, is deferred to Appendix~\ref{app:intermediate-support
         }. The overlay confirms Eq.~\eqref{eq:Fs} at the $10^{-3}$ level without interrupting the main derivation.
    325  	
        вӢ®
    854  		The framework unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric principle: total matrix-eleme
         nt weight is the trace-squared of the probe operator in the single-particle basis. Combined with band-structure
          input, it constrains different spin-triplet mechanisms at the same observed pairing symmetry. It invites syste
         matic tests across the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family under pressure and composition tuning and across differen
         t NMR-active sites within a single compound. A definitive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter
         -band pairing requires a multi-orbital extension of the specific anisotropic identities and protocol-level pock
         et/site bounds; the underlying Plancherel identity survives that extension unchanged.
    855 +
    856 +	\appendix
    857 +	
    858 +	\section{Supporting Checks for the Intermediate-SOC Interpolation}
    859 +	\label{app:intermediate-support}
    860 +	
    861 +	\subsection{Numerical cross-check against published Fig.~3}
    862 +	
    863 +	Extracting the $T=0$ intercepts of the five $s$-wave curves in Fig.~3 of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}, labeled by $
         gk_{F}/(k_{B}T_{c0})=0,1,2,4,30$, and converting via BCS $\Delta_{0}/(k_{B}T_{c0})=\pi/e^{\gamma_{E}}=1.7638769
         \ldots$,
    864 +	\begin{equation}
    865 +		\chi(0)/\chi_{N}=\tfrac{2}{3}F_{s}(gk_{F}/(k_{B}T_{c0})\cdot e^{\gamma_{E}}/\pi),
    866 +		\label{eq:fig3-crosscheck}
    867 +	\end{equation}
    868 +	yields the overlay in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig3-t0-validation}: max deviation $1.5\times 10^{-3}$ in $\chi/\chi_{N
         }$, RMS $7.7\times 10^{-4}$.
    869 +	
    870 +	\begin{figure}[t]
    871 +		\centering
    872 +		\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/fig3_t0_validation}
    873 +		\caption{Cross-check of Eq.~\eqref{eq:Fs} against the $T=0$ endpoints of the $s$-wave curves in Fig.~3
         of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}. Solid line: Eq.~\eqref{eq:fig3-crosscheck}. Symbols: extracted intercepts. Lower panel
         : digitized minus theory.}
    874 +		\label{fig:fig3-t0-validation}
    875 +	\end{figure}
    876 +	
    877 +	\subsection{Heuristic extension to unitary triplet with anisotropic gap}
    878 +	
    879 +	Replacing $\Delta\to|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|$ in the $\xi$-integration suggests the ansatz $F_{\rm t}(\lamb
         da_{\mathbf{k}})\approx F_{s}(|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|/|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|)$, with $F_{\rm t}\to 1$ at no
         des. This captures the gap-magnitude dependence exactly when the pairing amplitude is spatially constant across
          the FS, but misses corrections from the $\hat d$-vector angular structure and from intra-band triplet channels
         . A rigorous derivation is beyond the present scope.
    880  	0;в Ү X-Sum-Rule

вҖў IвҖҷve decided to keep the global architecture, but make the logic explicit inside it: clearer section titles, sharper
  transitions, and less jumping between вҖңgeneral,вҖқ вҖңspecial case,вҖқ and вҖңapplicationвҖқ without signposts. IвҖҷm reading the
  mid/late sections now before patching v11.0;в Ұ X-Sum-Rule

вҖў Edited SumRule-v11.tex (+55 -47)
     49  	
     50 -	Reference~\cite{pang2026} developed a self-consistent single-band Bogoliubov--de Gennes (BdG) theory for th
         e static uniform spin susceptibility $\chi_{\mu\nu}(T)$ of a superconductor under simultaneous Zeeman field and
          Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC)~\cite{Rashiba1960,Dresselhaus}. A key technical ingredient there is a ma
         trix-element sum rule (Eq.~(14) of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}) on the Bogoliubov transformation. In this paper we sho
         w that this sum rule is one member of a broader family and work out its physical consequences for NMR observabl
         es: Knight shift, its angular dependence, its response to applied field, and its dynamical partner $1/T_{1}$.
     50 +	Reference~\cite{pang2026} developed a self-consistent single-band Bogoliubov--de Gennes (BdG) theory for th
         e static uniform spin susceptibility $\chi_{\mu\nu}(T)$ of a superconductor under simultaneous Zeeman field and
          Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC)~\cite{Rashiba1960,Dresselhaus}. A key technical ingredient there is a ma
         trix-element sum rule (Eq.~(14) of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}) on the Bogoliubov transformation. In this paper we rec
         ast that sum rule as one member of a broader Plancherel family and use that reorganization to make the downstre
         am logic explicit. Once the exact identity is established, the remainder of the paper separates naturally into
         static $T=0$ consequences, controlled departures from the ideal strong-locking limit, and experimentally releva
         nt extensions to dynamics and multiple bands.
     51  	
     52 -	The paper is organized in two parts. \emph{Part~I} (Secs.~\ref{sec:setup}--\ref{sec:multiband}) develops th
         e theory. Section~\ref{sec:setup} fixes notation. Section~\ref{sec:generalized} establishes the generalized sum
          rule as a Plancherel identity; the spin-sector version of Ref.~\cite{pang2026} is one specialization, and char
         ge, multi-orbital, and site-resolved variants follow. Sections~\ref{sec:isoT0}--\ref{sec:anisoT0} specialize to
          the $T=0$ static spin susceptibility, yielding the trace bound and the anisotropic identity in the strong-lock
         ing regime. Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} presents the closed-form $F_{s}(\lambda)$ interpolation for $s$-wave
          pairing at arbitrary SOC. Section~\ref{sec:finiteH} extends the anisotropic identity to finite Zeeman field; a
          parity argument establishes quadratic stability of the zero-field result. Section~\ref{sec:spinFGT} derives th
         e spin analogue of the Ferrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule. Section~\ref{sec:multiband} gives the multi-band deco
         mposition.
     52 +	The paper is organized in two parts. \emph{Part~I} (Secs.~\ref{sec:setup}--\ref{sec:multiband}) develops th
         e theory in dependency order. Section~\ref{sec:setup} fixes notation. Section~\ref{sec:generalized} establishes
          the exact generalized sum rule as a Plancherel identity. Section~\ref{sec:isoT0} derives the simplest scalar c
         onsequence, an isotropic-FS trace bound. Section~\ref{sec:anisoT0} then trades isotropy for strong locking and
         yields the tensor identity that becomes the backbone of Part~II. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{sec:f
         initeH} relax one assumption at a time: the former keeps $\mathbf{H}=0$ but allows arbitrary SOC strength for $
         s$-wave pairing, while the latter restores finite Zeeman field but returns to strong locking. Section~\ref{sec:
         spinFGT} gives the dynamical counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} gives the structur
         al extension to multiple pockets.
     53  	
     54 -	\emph{Part~II} (Secs.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}--\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}) is an experimental manual. Section~\ref{sec:
         ellipsoid} states the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem: rotating the applied field on a single crystal at $T=0$ p
         roduces a quadric surface whose principal values, together with the trace constraint of Part~I, diagnose the pa
         iring class via a two-dimensional simplex. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} collects six self-contained measurement
         protocols covering trace-bound tests, ellipsoid diagnostics, finite-field SOC-texture spectroscopy, $1/T_{1}$ c
         ross-checks, site-resolved consistency, and pocket decomposition. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} applies the manual
          to K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ using the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. Section~\ref{sec
         :summary} summarizes.
     54 +	\emph{Part~II} (Secs.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}--\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}) converts those outputs into an experimental
         manual. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} states the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem: rotating the applied field on a
         single crystal at $T=0$ produces a quadric surface whose principal values, together with the trace constraint o
         f Part~I, diagnose the pairing class via a two-dimensional simplex. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the th
         eory into six measurement protocols ordered by experimental cost and theoretical input. Their dependency is sim
         ple: Protocol~A uses only the isotropic trace bound; Protocol~B uses the strong-locking tensor identity; Protoc
         ols~C--F add the finite-field, dynamical, site-resolved, and multiband ingredients from the later sections of P
         art~I. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ using the $^{75}$As data of Yang \
         emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. Section~\ref{sec:summary} summarizes.
     55  	
        вӢ®
     91  	
     92 -	\section{Generalized Bogoliubov matrix-element sum rule}
     92 +	\section[Exact Identity]{Exact identity: generalized Bogoliubov sum rule}
     93  	\label{sec:generalized}
     94  	
     95 -	We present the sum rule of Ref.~\cite{pang2026} as one specialization of a Plancherel-type identity on the
         Bogoliubov transformation. This framing makes the geometric content manifest and generates on demand the charge
         , multi-orbital, and site-resolved variants used later.
     95 +	Everything that follows rests on recasting the sum rule of Ref.~\cite{pang2026} as a Plancherel-type identi
         ty on the Bogoliubov transformation. This framing makes the geometric content manifest and generates on demand
         the charge, multi-orbital, and site-resolved variants used later.
     96  	
        вӢ®
    189  	
    190 -	\section{Trace sum rule on an isotropic Fermi surface}
    190 +	From here the logic branches in a controlled way. Sections~\ref{sec:isoT0} and~\ref{sec:anisoT0} extract th
         e two static $T=0$ consequences used most directly in experiment: first a scalar trace bound, then a full tenso
         r identity. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{sec:finiteH} relax one assumption at a time, while Secs.~\
         ref{sec:spinFGT} and~\ref{sec:multiband} extend the framework to dynamics and multiple pockets.
    191 +	
    192 +	\section[First Consequence]{First consequence: isotropic trace bound}
    193  	\label{sec:isoT0}
        вӢ®
    240  	
    239 -	\section{Anisotropic identity for a general Fermi surface and SOC}
    241 +	This isotropic trace bound is the lightest experimental entry point, but it is not yet the main geometric t
         heorem. The next section removes the spherical-FS assumption at the price of strong locking and yields the tens
         or identity that later becomes the ellipsoid diagnostic of Part~II.
    242 +	
    243 +	\section[Second Consequence]{Second consequence: strong-locking anisotropic identity}
    244  	\label{sec:anisoT0}
        вӢ®
    292  	
    289 -	\section{Intermediate-SOC interpolation for $s$-wave pairing}
    293 +	Equation~\eqref{eq:unified} is the main geometric theorem of the paper. The next two sections ask how it ch
         anges when one relaxes, one at a time, the two assumptions used above: infinite locking magnitude and zero appl
         ied field.
    294 +	
    295 +	\section[Controlled Departure I]{Controlled departure I: intermediate SOC}
    296  	\label{sec:intermediate}
    297  	
    292 -	At zero Zeeman field, the anisotropic identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified} admits an exact closed-form interpola

    326 -	\section{Strong-locking identity at finite Zeeman field}
    332 +	\section[Controlled Departure II]{Controlled departure II: finite Zeeman field}
    333  	\label{sec:finiteH}
    334  	
    329 -	The anisotropic identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified} was derived at $\mathbf{H}=0$. NMR experiments are necessar
         ily conducted at finite field, typically with $\mu_B H$ comparable to $\Delta$, so an extension is required. In
          this section we show that Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified} survives at finite $\mathbf{H}$ in the strong-locking regime
         $|\mathbf{b}_{\mathbf{k}}|\gg\Delta$, with a field-dependent projection tensor $\Pi(\mathbf{H})$; we quantify t
         he leading corrections and delineate the regimes in which a closed form is inaccessible. Throughout this sectio
         n we use the dimensionless ratios $\lambda\equiv|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|/\Delta$ and $h\equiv\mu_{B}|\mathbf{H
         }|/\Delta$.
    335 +	Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} relaxed the SOC-magnitude assumption while keeping $\mathbf{H}=0$. We now ta
         ke the complementary step: restore finite Zeeman field while returning to the strong-locking regime. In this se
         ction we show that Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified} survives at finite $\mathbf{H}$ in the strong-locking regime $|\mathb
         f{b}_{\mathbf{k}}|\gg\Delta$, with a field-dependent projection tensor $\Pi(\mathbf{H})$; we quantify the leadi
         ng corrections and delineate the regimes in which a closed form is inaccessible. Throughout this section we use
          the dimensionless ratios $\lambda\equiv|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|/\Delta$ and $h\equiv\mu_{B}|\mathbf{H}|/\Delt
         a$.

    401 -	\section{Spin analogue of the Ferrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule}
    407 +	\section[Dynamic Counterpart]{Dynamic counterpart: spin FGT sum rule}
    408  	\label{sec:spinFGT}
    409  	
    404 -	The results so far concern the static ($\omega=0$) spin susceptibility. A complementary identity constrains
          the \emph{dynamical} response: whatever Knight-shift deficit is produced by pair formation must reappear as ad
         ditional spectral weight at finite frequency in $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)$. We state this identity, t
         race its origin to the generalized sum rule of Sec.~\ref{sec:generalized}, and discuss its relation to the spin
         -lattice relaxation rate $1/T_{1}$.
    410 +	The preceding sections fixed the static geometry of $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)$. We now turn to its dynamical counte
         rpart: whatever Knight-shift deficit is produced by pair formation must reappear as additional spectral weight
         at finite frequency in $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)$. We state this identity, trace its origin to the ge
         neralized sum rule of Sec.~\ref{sec:generalized}, and discuss its relation to the spin-lattice relaxation rate
         $1/T_{1}$.

         re. The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of the orbital-diagonal assum
         ption; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-or
         bital derivation is therefore required before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalize
         d site/pocket bounds used there, to genuine inter-band pairing.

    524 -	\section*{Bridge: from identities to measurement}
    525 -	\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Bridge: from identities to measurement}
    532 +	\section*{Part II: From Identities to Measurement}
    533 +	\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Part II: From Identities to Measurement}
    534  	
    527 -	The remaining sections convert the identities of Part~I into experimental practice. The central geometric o
         bservation is that the strong-locking identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified}, $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}
         -\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is a linear map from a symmetric traceful tensor ($\Pi$, $\mathrm{Tr}\,\Pi=1$) to a measurable
         symmetric tensor ($K_{\mu\nu}(0)$). A single-crystal Knight-shift rotation at $T=0$ therefore determines $\Pi$
         directly, no fitting parameters. The eigenvalues of $\Pi$ live on a two-dimensional simplex, and each vertex, e
         dge, and interior point corresponds to a distinct pairing class. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} states this diagno
         stic. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} packages it, together with the finite-field, dynamical ($1/T_1$), site-resolv
         ed, and multi-band content of Part~I, into six experimental protocols. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} walks through
          K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ as a worked example.
    535 +	Part~I delivered four kinds of input: a scalar bound, a tensor geometry, controlled departures from the ide
         al strong-locking limit, and extensions to dynamics and multiple pockets. Part~II turns those outputs into meas
         urement logic. The central observation is that the strong-locking identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified}, $\chi_{\mu\n
         u}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is a linear map from a symmetric traceful tensor ($\Pi$, $\mathrm{T
         r}\,\Pi=1$) to a measurable symmetric tensor ($K_{\mu\nu}(0)$). A single-crystal Knight-shift rotation at $T=0$
          therefore determines $\Pi$ directly, without fitting parameters. The simplex organizes the result geometricall
         y; the protocol section then layers on the additional information from finite field, $1/T_1$, site resolution,
         and pocket decomposition. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} finally closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ as a wo
         rked example.
    536  	
    529 -	\section{Knight-shift ellipsoid and the simplex of pairing classes}
    537 +	\section[Core Diagnostic]{Core diagnostic: Knight-shift ellipsoid}

    540 +	This section is the conceptual core of Part~II. It turns Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified} into a directly measurable
         geometric object and then classifies the allowed shapes before the protocol section operationalizes the constru
         ction.
    541 +	
    542  	\subsection{Knight-shift tensor and ellipsoid representation}

         urements. Table~\ref{tab:protocols} summarizes what each protocol measures, its output, its assumptions, and th
         e signature of failure. The protocols are ordered from the lightest (a single three-axis Knight shift) to the m
         ost demanding (multi-site multi-field program).
    683 +	This section converts the geometric dictionary of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} into a hierarchy of measurements
         . Protocol~B is the central one; the others are either lighter entry points (Protocol~A) or targeted extensions
          and cross-checks (Protocols~C--F). Table~\ref{tab:protocols} summarizes what each protocol measures, its outpu
         t, its assumptions, and the signature of failure. The protocols are ordered from the lightest (a single three-a
         xis Knight shift) to the most demanding (multi-site multi-field program).

    851  		We distinguish two levels of conclusion. \emph{Robust experimental statement}: the Knight-shift tensor
         sits at the $\hat c$ vertex, and any acceptable microscopic theory must reproduce that near-vertex ellipsoid. \
         emph{Baseline theoretical inference}: within the orbital-diagonal framework of Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred}, th
         e data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline. A de
         finitive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter-band pairing requires a multi-orbital BdG analys
         is---the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} survives that extension unchanged, but the specifi
         c anisotropic identities and the pocket/site bounds used in Part~II require rederivation.
        вӢ®
    857  	
    848 -	The Bogoliubov transformation's unitarity encodes a Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} fro
         m which the spin-sector sum rule of Ref.~\cite{pang2026} is one specialization. Part~I developed the hierarchy
         of $T=0$ NMR-relevant consequences: the trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le 2\chi_{N}$ on a 3D isotropic FS (S
         ec.~\ref{sec:isoT0}); the FS-shape-independent strong-locking anisotropic identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\
         delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}); the closed-form interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)$ at arbit
         rary SOC, validated to $10^{-3}$ against existing numerics (Sec.~\ref{sec:intermediate}); its strong-locking fi
         nite-field extension $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0,\mathbf{H})=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}(\mathbf{H})]$ with quad
         ratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid (Sec.~\ref{sec:finiteH}); the spin-FGT sum rule tying Knight shift
         to integrated pair-breaking spectral weight (Sec.~\ref{sec:spinFGT}); and the multi-band decomposition with a s
         tructural prediction for the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family (Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband}).
    858 +	The paper was organized as a progression from one exact identity to experimentally usable diagnostics. Part
         ~I began with the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} and extracted two static $T=0$ consequenc
         es in increasing generality: the isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le 2\chi_{N}$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:is
         oT0}) and the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\P
         i_{\mu\nu}]$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}). These provide, respectively, the lightest scalar consistency test and th
         e full geometric kernel used in the experimental manual.
    859  	
    850 -	Part~II turned these identities into an experimental manual. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the Knight-
         shift ellipsoid theorem and the two-dimensional simplex of pairing classes, with a one-to-one correspondence be
         tween ellipsoid geometry and locking-vector texture (vertices=OSP/quasi-1D, edge-midpoints=ESP/2D Rashba, baryc
         enter=singlet strong SOC). Section~\ref{sec:protocols} collected six protocols of increasing sophistication: tr
         ace bound (A), full-ellipsoid diagnostic (B), finite-field SOC-texture spectroscopy (C), $1/T_{1}$ cross-check
         (D), site-resolved consistency (E), and pocket decomposition (F). The number of independent rotation angles req
         uired is set by crystal symmetry: two for uniaxial, three for orthorhombic, six for triclinic. Regimes outside
         strong locking, most notably the opening of a Bogoliubov Fermi surface, lie outside the closed-form reach of th
         e framework and are flagged as such.
    860 +	Part~I then treated controlled departures and extensions. Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} relaxed the infini
         te-locking assumption at $\mathbf{H}=0$ for $s$-wave pairing and produced the closed-form interpolation $F_{s}(
         \lambda)$, which contracts the strong-locking simplex radially. Section~\ref{sec:finiteH} restored finite Zeema
         n field within strong locking and showed quadratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid. Section~\ref{sec:spin
         FGT} supplied the dynamical spin-FGT counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} extended t
         he framework to multiple pockets, including the structural baseline for the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family.
    861  	
    852 -		Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} walked the protocols through the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{
         Triplet2021} on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ sits at the oblate-ax
         ial $\hat c$ vertex of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} and saturates the trace bound. Combined with the band-resolved DO
         S fractions reported there, the decoupled-pocket structural prediction of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband-A2Cr3As3} mis
         ses the observed vertex by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift units; within the orbital-diagonal baseline, t
         he data favor a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture
          mechanism. The observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot originate from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-
         FGT budget and is a direct fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation, consistent wit
         h the Curie-Weiss enhancement of $1/T_{1}T$ above $T_{c}$.
    853 -	
    854 -		The framework unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric principle: total matrix-eleme
         nt weight is the trace-squared of the probe operator in the single-particle basis. Combined with band-structure
          input, it constrains different spin-triplet mechanisms at the same observed pairing symmetry. It invites syste
         matic tests across the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family under pressure and composition tuning and across differen
         t NMR-active sites within a single compound. A definitive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter
         -band pairing requires a multi-orbital extension of the specific anisotropic identities and protocol-level pock
         et/site bounds; the underlying Plancherel identity survives that extension unchanged.
    862 +	Part~II translated these outputs into measurement logic. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the ellipsoid t
         heorem and pairing simplex; Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} organized six protocols ordered by experimental cost and t
         heoretical input; Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closed the loop on the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Tri
         plet2021}. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ fixes the Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c$ ve
         rtex and saturates the trace bound. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the decoupled-pocket SOC-texture pred
         iction misses the data by $\sim 0.5$, so the data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$ interpretation over the independe
         nt-pocket SOC-texture baseline, while the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$
          FM spin-fluctuation gap formation. The framework therefore unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single g
         eometric principle and makes explicit which parts of the inference are robust experimentally and which remain b
         aseline-theoretical pending a genuine multi-orbital extension.

     39 -	\begin{abstract}
     40 -		We present a unified sum-rule framework for the NMR response of single-band noncentrosymmetric supercon
         ductors in arbitrary Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC), organized in two parts. \emph{Part
         ~I} (theory) establishes the framework. A Plancherel identity $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+\sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr
         }_{s}(O^{2})$, valid for every Hermitian single-particle operator $O$, underlies (i) a trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}
         \,\chi(0)\le\tfrac{2}{3}\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T_{c})=2\chi_{N}$ on a 3D isotropic Fermi surface; (ii) a strong-lock
         ing anisotropic identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$ with the locking projector $
         \Pi_{\mu\nu}=\langle\hat n_{\mu}\hat n_{\nu}\rangle_{\rm FS}$; (iii) a closed-form $s$-wave interpolation $F_{s
         }(\lambda)=1-\sinh^{-1}\lambda/[\lambda\sqrt{1+\lambda^{2}}]$ at arbitrary SOC, validated against existing nume
         rics to $10^{-3}$; (iv) a finite-field extension $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0,\mathbf{H})=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu
         \nu}(\mathbf{H})]$ stable to $O(h^{2}/\lambda^{2})$ against small applied fields; (v) a spin analogue of the Fe
         rrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule tying the Knight-shift deficit to integrated pair-breaking spectral weight; and
          (vi) the multi-band decomposition. \emph{Part~II} (manual) converts these identities into experimental practic
         e: the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem and the two-dimensional simplex of pairing classes (one-to-one map from e
         llipsoid geometry to locking texture); a hyperfine-invariant site-resolved bound as an internal consistency che
         ck; and six protocols covering trace-bound tests, full-ellipsoid diagnostics, finite-field SOC-texture spectros
         copy, $1/T_{1}$ cross-checks, site-resolved tests, and pocket decomposition. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data
         on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, the framework places the observed projection at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$,
         saturates the trace bound, and, within the orbital-diagonal baseline, favors a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\paralle
         l\hat c$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture one, while identifying the suppression of $1/T_
         {1}\parallel\hat c$ as a fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation gap formation. Regim
         es outside strong locking, most notably Bogoliubov-Fermi-surface formation, are explicitly outside the closed-f
         orm reach of the framework.
     41 -	\end{abstract}
     39 +		\begin{abstract}
     40 +			We present a two-part framework for the NMR response of single-band noncentrosymmetric superconduct
         ors in Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC). \emph{Part~I} starts from a Plancherel identity,
          $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+\sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr}_{s}(O^{2})$, valid for every Hermitian single-particle oper
         ator $O$. From it we derive an isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le\tfrac{2}{3}\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T
         _{c})=2\chi_{N}$, the strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$,
          a closed-form $s$-wave interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)=1-\sinh^{-1}\lambda/[\lambda\sqrt{1+\lambda^{2}}]$ at arb
         itrary SOC validated against existing numerics, its finite-field strong-locking extension stable to $O(h^{2}/\l
         ambda^{2})$ against small applied fields, a spin analogue of the Ferrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule, and a decou
         pled multiband baseline. \emph{Part~II} turns these outputs into measurement logic: the Knight-shift ellipsoid
         theorem and simplex of pairing classes, a hyperfine-invariant site-resolved bound, and six protocols ranging fr
         om the trace-bound test to pocket decomposition. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$,
         the observed point lies at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$, saturates the trace bound, disfavors the independ
         ent-pocket SOC-texture baseline in favor of a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ interpretation within th
         e orbital-diagonal setting, and identifies the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ as a fingerprint of fini
         te-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation gap formation. Regimes outside strong locking, most notably Bogo
         liubov-Fermi-surface formation, remain outside the closed-form reach of the framework.
     41 +		\end{abstract}
     42

     50 -	Reference~\cite{pang2026} developed a self-consistent single-band Bogoliubov--de Gennes (BdG) theory for th
         e static uniform spin susceptibility $\chi_{\mu\nu}(T)$ of a superconductor under simultaneous Zeeman field and
          Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC)~\cite{Rashiba1960,Dresselhaus}. A key technical ingredient there is a ma
         trix-element sum rule (Eq.~(14) of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}) on the Bogoliubov transformation. In this paper we rec
         ast that sum rule as one member of a broader Plancherel family and use that reorganization to make the downstre
         am logic explicit. Once the exact identity is established, the remainder of the paper separates naturally into
         static $T=0$ consequences, controlled departures from the ideal strong-locking limit, and experimentally releva
         nt extensions to dynamics and multiple bands.
     50 +		Reference~\cite{pang2026} developed a self-consistent single-band Bogoliubov--de Gennes (BdG) theory fo
         r the static uniform spin susceptibility $\chi_{\mu\nu}(T)$ of a superconductor under simultaneous Zeeman field
          and Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC)~\cite{Rashiba1960,Dresselhaus}. A key technical ingredient there is
         a matrix-element sum rule (Eq.~(14) of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}) on the Bogoliubov transformation. In this paper we
          elevate that relation to one member of a broader Plancherel family and organize the manuscript around its cons
         equences. The logical spine is simple: exact identity $\rightarrow$ static $T=0$ consequences $\rightarrow$ con
         trolled departures and extensions $\rightarrow$ experimental manual.

         .377$, $F_{s}(2)=0.677$, $F_{s}(5)=0.909$; half-saturation at $\lambda\approx 1.317$.
