         ng a pair-breaking spectroscopy of $|\mathbf{g}|/\Delta$ from three-axis Knight shift (deployed as Protocol~B i

    325 +		\subsection{Geometric interpretation and SOC spectroscopy}
    326 +		
    327 +		Equation~\eqref{eq:chi-explicit} shows that intermediate SOC does not replace the strong-locking tensor

    328 +		\[
    329 +		\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)/\chi_{N}=2\langle F_{s}(\lambda_{\mathbf{k}})\rangle_{\rm FS}\le 2,

    331 +		strict for finite SOC. For uniform SOC the relation is invertible: $\lambda=F_{s}^{-1}(\mathrm{Tr}\,\ch
         i(0)/2\chi_{N})$, yielding a pair-breaking spectroscopy of $|\mathbf{g}|/\Delta$ from three-axis Knight shift (
         deployed as Protocol~B in Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols}).
    332 +		
    333 +		Operationally, this section supplies the radial contraction away from the strong-locking simplex used l
         ater in Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}: for uniform SOC magnitude, $\Pi^{\rm eff}=F_{s}(\lambda)\Pi$, so every strong
         -locking point moves along a straight line toward the origin as SOC weakens. An independent numerical overlay a
         gainst the published BdG curves of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}, together with a brief heuristic note on anisotropic-ga
         p unitary triplets, is deferred to Appendix~\ref{app:intermediate-support}. The overlay confirms Eq.~\eqref{eq:
         Fs} at the $10^{-3}$ level without interrupting the main derivation.

         1)=0.377$, $F_{s}(2)=0.677$, $F_{s}(5)=0.909$; half-saturation at $\lambda\approx 1.317$.

    405 -	valid both at low field ($\mu_B H\ll gk_F$, covered by Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi-H-weak}) and high field ($\mu_B H\g
         g gk_F$, Eq.~\eqref{eq:strongZeeman}), but not in the crossover window around $H^{\ast}$. Outside the strong-lo
         cking regime, no closed form is known. We verified numerically that naive substitutions such as $\Delta\to\sqrt

         ng by $O(1)$ in $\chi/\chi_N$ across the bulk of the $(g,H)$ phase diagram; the problem is structural (the BdG
         fails to block-diagonalize) rather than a matter of substitution.

    412 +		valid both at low field ($\mu_B H\ll gk_F$, covered by Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi-H-weak}) and high field ($\mu_B
          H\gg gk_F$, Eq.~\eqref{eq:strongZeeman}), but not in the crossover window around $H^{\ast}$. Outside the stron
         g-locking regime, no closed form is known. We verified numerically that naive substitutions such as $\Delta\to\
         sqrt{\Delta^2-(\mu_B H)^2}$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:chi-explicit} do not reproduce the self-consistent BdG result, dep
         arting by $O(1)$ in $\chi/\chi_N$ across the bulk of the $(g,H)$ phase diagram; the problem is structural (the
         BdG fails to block-diagonalize) rather than a matter of substitution. Protocol~C in Part~II is built precisely
         around this regime separation: simple ellipsoid deformation away from the crossover, full numerics near $H^{\as
         t}$.

    416  	
    410 -	The preceding sections fixed the static geometry of $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)$. We now turn to its dynamical counte
         rpart: whatever Knight-shift deficit is produced by pair formation must reappear as additional spectral weight
         at finite frequency in $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)$. We state this identity, trace its origin to the ge
         neralized sum rule of Sec.~\ref{sec:generalized}, and discuss its relation to the spin-lattice relaxation rate
         $1/T_{1}$.
    417 +		The preceding sections fixed the static geometry of $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)$. We now turn to its dynamical co
         unterpart: whatever Knight-shift deficit is produced by pair formation must reappear as additional spectral wei
         ght at finite frequency in $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)$. This section supplies the only additional inpu
         t needed for the $1/T_{1}$ cross-check of Part~II: a budget relation between the static ellipsoid and dynamical
          spectral-weight transfer.

    496 -	\label{sec:multiband}

    498 -	The last extension of Part~I changes not the control parameters but the Hilbert-space structure. We now lif
         t the single-pocket assumption and ask how the geometric identities organize materials with several FS sheets.
    502 +		\section[Structural Extension]{Structural extension: decoupled multiband baseline}
    503 +		\label{sec:multiband}
    504 +		
    505 +		The last extension of Part~I changes not the control parameters but the Hilbert-space structure. Becaus
         e the fully general multi-orbital problem is broader than the present paper, we isolate the piece that Part~II
         can use directly: the decoupled-pocket baseline in which each FS sheet contributes additively and deviations ca
         n be read as signatures of inter-band structure.
    506  	
        вӢ®
    519  	
    513 -	\subsection{Inter-band mixing}
    514 -	
    515 -	Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} neglects inter-band pairing (exact for orbital-diagonal pairing). For orbi
         tally mixed pairing, the formula is a decoupled-pocket baseline; deviations diagnose the inter-band structure.
         The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of the orbital-diagonal assumptio
         n; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbita
         l derivation is therefore required before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalized si
         te/pocket bounds used there, to genuine inter-band pairing.
    520 +		\subsection{Scope: inter-band pairing as deviation from baseline}
    521 +		
    522 +		Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} is exact for orbital-diagonal pairing. For orbitally mixed pairing it
         becomes a baseline rather than a theorem: deviations diagnose the inter-band structure. The Plancherel identity
          Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of the orbital-diagonal assumption; it is the specific a
         nisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbital derivation is therefo
         re required before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalized site/pocket bounds used t
         here, to genuine inter-band pairing.

         \tfrac{1}{3},\tfrac{1}{3})$ to the vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the simplex of Part~II (Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex}). This on

    540  	
    534 -	Part~I delivered four kinds of input: a scalar bound, a tensor geometry, controlled departures from the ide
         al strong-locking limit, and extensions to dynamics and multiple pockets. Part~II turns those outputs into meas
         urement logic. The central observation is that the strong-locking identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified}, $\chi_{\mu\n
         u}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is a linear map from a symmetric traceful tensor ($\Pi$, $\mathrm{T
         r}\,\Pi=1$) to a measurable symmetric tensor ($K_{\mu\nu}(0)$). A single-crystal Knight-shift rotation at $T=0$
          therefore determines $\Pi$ directly, without fitting parameters. The simplex organizes the result geometricall
         y; the protocol section then layers on the additional information from finite field, $1/T_1$, site resolution,
         and pocket decomposition. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} finally closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ as a wo
         rked example.
    541 +		Part~I produced four kinds of input: a scalar bound, a tensor geometry, controlled departures from the
         ideal strong-locking limit, and structural extensions to dynamics and multiple pockets. Part~II uses them in th
         e same order an experiment would. First, the strong-locking identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified}, $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)/
         \chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is read directly as a map from the measurable Knight-shift tensor to the
         projection tensor $\Pi$; this is the ellipsoid theorem of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}. Next, Sec.~\ref{sec:protoco
         ls} packages the remaining ingredients as successive layers of inference: start from the trace bound when only
         limited data exist, upgrade to the full ellipsoid when rotation data are available, then add finite field, $1/T
         _1$, site resolution, and pocket decomposition only when needed. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} finally applies tha
         t manual to K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ in the same order.

    684 +		For uniform SOC magnitude, $\Pi^{\rm eff}=F_{s}(\lambda)\Pi$, so $p_{i}=F_{s}(\lambda)\pi_{i}$: the str

         _{1},p_{2},p_{3})$. The next section turns those coordinates into an ordered measurement program.
    687 +		
    688 +		\section[Experimental Manual]{Experimental manual: six protocols}
    689 +		\label{sec:protocols}
    690 +		
    691 +		This section converts the geometric dictionary of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} into an ordered deployment g
         uide. If only three orthogonal Knight shifts are available, start with Protocol~A. If a full single-crystal rot
         ation is available, Protocol~B becomes the central measurement because it determines the ellipsoid directly. Pr
         otocols~C--F then add one extra layer each: field dependence, dynamical cross-checks, site consistency, and poc
         ket decomposition. Table~\ref{tab:protocols} summarizes what each protocol measures, its output, its assumption
         s, and the signature of failure.
    692  	
    679 -	\section{Experimental protocols}
    680 -	\label{sec:protocols}
    681 -	
    682 -	This section converts the geometric dictionary of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} into a hierarchy of measurements
         . Protocol~B is the central one; the others are either lighter entry points (Protocol~A) or targeted extensions
          and cross-checks (Protocols~C--F). Table~\ref{tab:protocols} summarizes what each protocol measures, its outpu
         t, its assumptions, and the signature of failure. The protocols are ordered from the lightest (a single three-a
         xis Knight shift) to the most demanding (multi-site multi-field program).

    746  	
    738 -	At finite field, the ellipsoid deforms according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:strongH-identity}; rotating $\hat{\mathbf
         {H}}$ at fixed $H$ traces a family of points on the simplex that is a parameter-free prediction once the SOC te
         xture is known.
    747 +		Protocol~C is worth attempting only after Protocol~B has identified a strong-locking ellipsoid at low f
         ield. It asks how that ellipsoid deforms under an applied field according to Eq.~\eqref{eq:strongH-identity}.
    748  	
        вӢ®
    760  	
    752 -	Combining Knight-shift geometry with $1/T_{1}$ along the same field orientations tests the $\mathbf{q}\appr
         ox 0$ spin-FGT budget of Eq.~\eqref{eq:T1-bound}.
    761 +		Protocol~D is a cross-check rather than a standalone classifier: it asks whether the dynamical spectral
         -weight loss seen in $1/T_{1}$ is consistent with the static ellipsoid already fixed by Protocol~B.
    762  	
        вӢ®
    778  	
    770 -	The site-resolved bound Eq.~\eqref{eq:site-bound} is hyperfine-invariant: different nuclei in the same comp
         ound must yield the same normalized Knight-shift geometry. This is the strongest internal consistency check ava
         ilable from a single crystal.
    779 +		Protocol~E tests whether the same geometric kernel is seen by different nuclei. The site-resolved bound
          Eq.~\eqref{eq:site-bound} is hyperfine-invariant, so different nuclei in the same compound must yield the same
          normalized Knight-shift geometry.
    780  	
        вӢ®
    791  	
    783 -	For materials with multiple FS pockets, the multi-band decomposition Eq.~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} admits a d
         irect experimental test under the orbital-diagonal hypothesis. The application to A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ with
         the textures of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband-A2Cr3As3} gives Eqs.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-perp}--\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-par}.
    792 +		Protocol~F is the last layer. It interprets the observed ellipsoid within the decoupled-pocket baseline
          of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} and is meaningful only after the lower-level geometry has already been fixed. The
         application to A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ with the textures of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband-A2Cr3As3} gives Eqs.~\eqref
         {eq:a2cr3as3-perp}--\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-par}.
    793  	
        вӢ®
    804  	
    796 -	With the geometric dictionary and protocol hierarchy in place, we now close the loop on a concrete material
         . We walk through the protocols of Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} using the $^{75}$As Knight-shift and $1/T_{1}$ data
          of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021} on single-crystal K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$. Three findings follow: (a)
          the observed ellipsoid sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the simplex; (b) the band-resolved SOC-tex
         ture prediction Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} disagrees with the observation by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-
         shift units, favoring a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector mechanism within the orbital-diagonal baseline; (c) the obse
         rved suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT budget and diagnoses
         finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation.
    797 -	
    798 -	\subsection{Inputs and direct diagnostic}
    799 -	\label{sec:K2Cr3As3-inputs}
    805 +		With the manual in place, we now close the loop on a concrete material. We apply the protocols of Sec.~
         \ref{sec:protocols} to the $^{75}$As Knight-shift and $1/T_{1}$ data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021} o
         n single-crystal K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ in the same order as the logic developed above: Protocol~B fixes the e
         llipsoid, Protocol~F tests the decoupled-pocket baseline, and Protocol~D uses the relaxation data to diagnose t
         he missing dynamics. Three findings follow: (a) the observed ellipsoid sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)
         $ of the simplex; (b) the band-resolved SOC-texture baseline Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} disagrees with the ob
         servation by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift units, favoring a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector mechanism withi
         n the orbital-diagonal setting; (c) the observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from the $
         \mathbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT budget and diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation.
    806 +

    808 +		\label{sec:K2Cr3As3-inputs}

    811 -		The observed point sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} within experimen
         tal uncertainty, and $\sum\pi_{i}=1\pm O(\epsilon)$ saturates the universal constraint Eq.~\eqref{eq:trace-from
         -ellipsoid}. At the level of the measured ellipsoid, Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi} fixes the effective locking tex
         ture to the $\hat c$ vertex of the simplex. Within the canonical single-band catalogue of Table~\ref{tab:ellips
         oids}, this matches the \emph{OSP unitary spin-triplet with $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$} limit, or equivalently
          the quasi-1D $\hat n\parallel\hat c$ vertex. This reproduces---via the ellipsoid diagnostic of Protocol~B, wit
         hout invoking an $\mathbf{H}$-$T$ phase diagram---the principal conclusion of Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} at the le
         vel of the strong-locking single-band framework.

    820 +			The observed point sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} within exper
         imental uncertainty, and $\sum\pi_{i}=1\pm O(\epsilon)$ saturates the universal constraint Eq.~\eqref{eq:trace-
         from-ellipsoid}. At the protocol level, this is the complete output of Protocol~B: the measured ellipsoid fixes
          the effective locking texture to the $\hat c$ vertex of the simplex. Within the canonical single-band catalogu
         e of Table~\ref{tab:ellipsoids}, this matches the \emph{OSP unitary spin-triplet with $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat
         c$} limit, or equivalently the quasi-1D $\hat n\parallel\hat c$ vertex. The main experimental statement therefo
         re precedes any multiband modeling: the Knight-shift tensor itself already selects the $\hat c$ vertex.
    821 +		
    822 +		\subsection{Protocol F cross-check: tension with the band-resolved SOC-texture baseline}
    823 +		\label{sec:K2Cr3As3-bandtension}
    824 +		
    825 +		Protocol~F now asks whether the decoupled-pocket baseline of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} can reproduce the
          observed vertex. The multiband structural prediction Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} assumes quasi-1D SOC along $
         \hat c$ on the $\alpha,\beta$ bands and approximately isotropic Rashba SOC on the 3D $\gamma$ band. Reference~\
         cite{Triplet2021} quotes a DFT value $w_{\gamma}\approx 0.75$ for K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, i.e., $w_{\parallel}
         =1-w_{\gamma}\approx 0.25$. Substituting,

        вӢ®
    849  	
    841 -	\subsection{Verdict and caveats}
    842 -	\label{sec:K2Cr3As3-verdict}
    843 -	
    844 -	Three pieces of evidence from Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} are organized under the framework:
    850 +		\subsection{Interpretation and scope}
    851 +		\label{sec:K2Cr3As3-verdict}
    852 +		
    853 +		Putting the three protocols together separates what is fixed directly by experiment from what remains a
          baseline theoretical inference. Three pieces of evidence from Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} are organized under the
         framework:
    854  		\begin{enumerate}
        вӢ®
    865  	
    857 -	The paper was organized as a progression from one exact identity to experimentally usable diagnostics. Part
         ~I began with the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} and extracted two static $T=0$ consequenc
         es in increasing generality: the isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le 2\chi_{N}$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:is
         oT0}) and the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\P
         i_{\mu\nu}]$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}). These provide, respectively, the lightest scalar consistency test and th
         e full geometric kernel used in the experimental manual.
    858 -	
    859 -	Part~I then treated controlled departures and extensions. Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} relaxed the infini
         te-locking assumption at $\mathbf{H}=0$ for $s$-wave pairing and produced the closed-form interpolation $F_{s}(
         \lambda)$, which contracts the strong-locking simplex radially. Section~\ref{sec:finiteH} restored finite Zeema
         n field within strong locking and showed quadratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid. Section~\ref{sec:spin
         FGT} supplied the dynamical spin-FGT counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} extended t
         he framework to multiple pockets, including the structural baseline for the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family.
    860 -	
    861 -	Part~II translated these outputs into measurement logic. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the ellipsoid t
         heorem and pairing simplex; Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} organized six protocols ordered by experimental cost and t
         heoretical input; Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closed the loop on the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Tri
         plet2021}. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ fixes the Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c$ ve
         rtex and saturates the trace bound. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the decoupled-pocket SOC-texture pred
         iction misses the data by $\sim 0.5$, so the data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$ interpretation over the independe
         nt-pocket SOC-texture baseline, while the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$
          FM spin-fluctuation gap formation. The framework therefore unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single g
         eometric principle and makes explicit which parts of the inference are robust experimentally and which remain b
         aseline-theoretical pending a genuine multi-orbital extension.
    866 +		The paper was organized as a progression from one exact identity to experimentally usable diagnostics.
         Part~I began with the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} and extracted two static $T=0$ conseq
         uences in increasing generality: the isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le 2\chi_{N}$ (Sec.~\ref{se
         c:isoT0}) and the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu
         }-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}). These provide, respectively, the lightest scalar consistency test an
         d the full geometric kernel that Part~II reads out experimentally.
    867 +		
    868 +		Part~I then treated controlled departures and extensions. Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} relaxed the in
         finite-locking assumption at $\mathbf{H}=0$ for $s$-wave pairing and produced the closed-form interpolation $F_
         {s}(\lambda)$, which contracts the strong-locking simplex radially. Section~\ref{sec:finiteH} restored finite Z
         eeman field within strong locking and showed quadratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid. Section~\ref{sec:
         spinFGT} supplied the dynamical spin-FGT counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} isolat
         ed the decoupled-pocket multiband baseline used later as an experimental comparison point.
    869 +		
    870 +		Part~II then used those outputs in measurement order. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the ellipsoid
         theorem and pairing simplex; Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} organized six protocols from the lightest scalar check to
          the multiband baseline test; Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} applied that manual to the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{e
         t al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ fixes the Knight-shift ellipsoid at
          the $\hat c$ vertex and saturates the trace bound. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the decoupled-pocket
         SOC-texture prediction misses the data by $\sim 0.5$, so the data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$ interpretation ov
         er the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline, while the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ diagnoses fin
         ite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation. The framework therefore unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$
         under a single geometric principle and makes explicit which parts of the inference are robust experimentally an
         d which remain baseline-theoretical pending a genuine multi-orbital extension.

вҖў Edited SumRule-v12.tex (+2 -2)
    51  	
    52 -	The paper is organized in two parts. \emph{Part~I} (Secs.~\ref{sec:setup}--\ref{sec:multiband}) develops the
         theory in dependency order. Section~\ref{sec:setup} fixes notation. Section~\ref{sec:generalized} establishes t
        he exact generalized sum rule as a Plancherel identity. Section~\ref{sec:isoT0} derives the simplest scalar cons
        equence, an isotropic-FS trace bound. Section~\ref{sec:anisoT0} then trades isotropy for strong locking and yiel
        ds the tensor identity that becomes the backbone of Part~II. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{sec:finite
        H} relax one assumption at a time: the former keeps $\mathbf{H}=0$ but allows arbitrary SOC strength for $s$-wav
        e pairing, while the latter restores finite Zeeman field but returns to strong locking. Section~\ref{sec:spinFGT
        } gives the dynamical counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} gives the structural exten
        sion to multiple pockets.
    52 +		The paper is organized in two parts. \emph{Part~I} (Secs.~\ref{sec:setup}--\ref{sec:multiband}) develops
         the theory in dependency order. Section~\ref{sec:setup} fixes notation. Section~\ref{sec:generalized} establish
        es the exact generalized sum rule as a Plancherel identity. Section~\ref{sec:isoT0} derives the simplest scalar
        consequence, an isotropic-FS trace bound. Section~\ref{sec:anisoT0} then trades isotropy for strong locking and
        yields the tensor identity that becomes the backbone of Part~II. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{sec:fi
        niteH} relax one assumption at a time: the former keeps $\mathbf{H}=0$ but allows arbitrary SOC strength for $s$
        -wave pairing, while the latter restores finite Zeeman field but returns to strong locking. Section~\ref{sec:spi
        nFGT} gives the dynamical counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} isolates the decoupled
         multiband baseline used later in the experimental manual.
    53  	
    54 -	\emph{Part~II} (Secs.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}--\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}) converts those outputs into an experimental m
        anual. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} states the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem: rotating the applied field on a si
        ngle crystal at $T=0$ produces a quadric surface whose principal values, together with the trace constraint of P
        art~I, diagnose the pairing class via a two-dimensional simplex. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the theory
         into six measurement protocols ordered by experimental cost and theoretical input. Their dependency is simple:
        Protocol~A uses only the isotropic trace bound; Protocol~B uses the strong-locking tensor identity; Protocols~C-
        -F add the finite-field, dynamical, site-resolved, and multiband ingredients from the later sections of Part~I.
        Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ using the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et
        al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. Section~\ref{sec:summary} summarizes.
    54 +		\emph{Part~II} (Secs.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}--\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}) converts those outputs into an experiment
        al manual. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} states the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem: rotating the applied field on
        a single crystal at $T=0$ produces a quadric surface whose principal values, together with the trace constraint
        of Part~I, diagnose the pairing class via a two-dimensional simplex. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the th
        eory into six measurement protocols ordered by experimental cost and theoretical input. Their dependency is simp
        le: Protocol~A uses only the isotropic trace bound; Protocol~B uses the strong-locking tensor identity; Protocol
        s~C--F add the finite-field, dynamical, site-resolved, and multiband-baseline ingredients from the later section
        s of Part~I. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ using the $^{75}$As data of Y
        ang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. Section~\ref{sec:summary} summarizes.
    55  	Wog0;в № X-Sum-Rule

вҖў Edited SumRule-v13.tex (+34 -31)
    446  	
    447 -	\subsection{Physical content: spectral-weight transfer}
    448 -	
    449 -	Figure~\ref{fig:spinFGT-spectral} sketches the ph and pp pieces of the spectral function. In the normal sta
         te, the Pauli component appears at $\omega\to 0$ (Landau-broadened at finite $T$), with Van Vleck weight at the
          SOC-induced interband threshold. In the SC state, the $\omega\to 0$ weight is reduced by the factor $1-\pi_{\m
         u}$, and the displaced weight reappears above $2\Delta_{\min}$ as pair-breaking absorption. The total integrate
         d $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi/\omega$ is preserved.
    450 -	
    451 -	\begin{figure}[t]
    452 -		\centering
    453 -		\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]
    454 -			\draw[->] (0,0) -- (7,0) node[right] {$\omega$};
    455 -			\draw[->] (0,0) -- (0,3) node[above] {$\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$};
    456 -			\draw[thick,blue] (0,0) -- (0.02,2.6);
    457 -			\draw[thick,blue] (0.02,2.6) -- (0.2,0.2);
    458 -			\draw[thick,blue] (0.2,0.2) -- (4,0.2) .. controls (4.5,0.5) and (5,0.5) .. (5.5,0.2) -- (7,0.2);
    459 -			\node[blue] at (1,2.3) {\footnotesize normal};
    460 -			\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0,0) -- (0.02,1.4);
    461 -			\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0.02,1.4) -- (0.2,0.1);

    463 -			\draw[thick,red,dashed] (2.5,0.1) .. controls (3,0.8) and (3.5,0.9) .. (4.5,0.6) .. controls (5.2,0
         .4) and (6,0.3) .. (7,0.25);
    464 -			\node[red] at (4.5,1.1) {\footnotesize SC};
    465 -			\draw[dashed,gray] (2.5,0) -- (2.5,1);
    466 -			\node[below] at (2.5,-0.05) {\footnotesize $2\Delta_{\min}$};
    467 -			\draw[<->, thick, black] (-0.4,1.4) -- (-0.4,2.6);

    470 -		\caption{Schematic: $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$ in the normal state (blue) and the SC s
         tate (red). Spectral weight of magnitude $\chi_{N}\pi_{\mu}$ is transferred from the static peak at $\omega\to
         0$ into the pair-breaking continuum above $2\Delta_{\min}$, conserving the Kramers--Kronig integral Eq.~\eqref{
         eq:spinFGT}. The Van Vleck bump at the interband threshold is essentially unaffected by pairing at leading orde
         r in $\Delta/|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|$.}
    471 -		\label{fig:spinFGT-spectral}
    472 -	\end{figure}
    447 +		\subsection{Physical content: spectral-weight transfer}
    448 +		
    449 +		The content of Eq.~\eqref{eq:spinFGT} is simple. In the normal state, the Pauli component sits at $\ome
         ga\to 0$ (Landau-broadened at finite $T$), with additional Van Vleck weight at the SOC-induced interband thresh
         old. In the superconducting state, the $\omega\to 0$ weight is reduced by the factor $1-\pi_{\mu}$, and the dis
         placed weight reappears above $2\Delta_{\min}$ as pair-breaking absorption. The total integrated $\mathrm{Im}\,
         \chi/\omega$ is preserved. A schematic is deferred to Appendix~\ref{app:spinFGT-support} so that the main text
         can keep the dynamical argument on the same footing as the static derivation.
    450  	
        вӢ®
    868  	\par
    892 -	
    893 -	\subsection{Heuristic extension to unitary triplet with anisotropic gap}
    894 -	
    895 -	Replacing $\Delta\to|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|$ in the $\xi$-integration suggests the ansatz $F_{\rm t}(\lamb
         da_{\mathbf{k}})\approx F_{s}(|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|/|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|)$, with $F_{\rm t}\to 1$ at no
         des. This captures the gap-magnitude dependence exactly when the pairing amplitude is spatially constant across
          the FS, but misses corrections from the $\hat d$-vector angular structure and from intra-band triplet channels
         . A rigorous derivation is beyond the present scope.
    869  	
    897 -	\clearpage
    870 +		\subsection{Heuristic extension to unitary triplet with anisotropic gap}
    871 +		
    872 +		Replacing $\Delta\to|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|$ in the $\xi$-integration suggests the ansatz $F_{\rm t}(\
         lambda_{\mathbf{k}})\approx F_{s}(|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|/|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|)$, with $F_{\rm t}\to 1$ a
         t nodes. This captures the gap-magnitude dependence exactly when the pairing amplitude is spatially constant ac
         ross the FS, but misses corrections from the $\hat d$-vector angular structure and from intra-band triplet chan
         nels. A rigorous derivation is beyond the present scope.
    873 +		
    874 +		\section{Schematic for the Spin FGT Interpretation}
    875 +		\label{app:spinFGT-support}
    876 +		
    877 +		\begin{figure}[h]
    878 +			\centering
    879 +			\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]
    880 +				\draw[->] (0,0) -- (7,0) node[right] {$\omega$};
    881 +				\draw[->] (0,0) -- (0,3) node[above] {$\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$};
    882 +				\draw[thick,blue] (0,0) -- (0.02,2.6);
    883 +				\draw[thick,blue] (0.02,2.6) -- (0.2,0.2);
    884 +				\draw[thick,blue] (0.2,0.2) -- (4,0.2) .. controls (4.5,0.5) and (5,0.5) .. (5.5,0.2) -- (7,0.2
         );
    885 +				\node[blue] at (1,2.3) {\footnotesize normal};
    886 +				\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0,0) -- (0.02,1.4);
    887 +				\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0.02,1.4) -- (0.2,0.1);
    888 +				\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0.2,0.1) -- (2.5,0.1);
    889 +				\draw[thick,red,dashed] (2.5,0.1) .. controls (3,0.8) and (3.5,0.9) .. (4.5,0.6) .. controls (5
         .2,0.4) and (6,0.3) .. (7,0.25);
    890 +				\node[red] at (4.5,1.1) {\footnotesize SC};
    891 +				\draw[dashed,gray] (2.5,0) -- (2.5,1);
    892 +				\node[below] at (2.5,-0.05) {\footnotesize $2\Delta_{\min}$};
    893 +				\draw[<->, thick, black] (-0.4,1.4) -- (-0.4,2.6);
    894 +				\node[left] at (-0.4,2.0) {\footnotesize $\chi_N\pi_\mu$};
    895 +			\end{tikzpicture}
    896 +			\caption{Schematic: $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$ in the normal state (blue) and the
         SC state (red). Spectral weight of magnitude $\chi_{N}\pi_{\mu}$ is transferred from the static peak at $\omega
         \to 0$ into the pair-breaking continuum above $2\Delta_{\min}$, conserving the Kramers--Kronig integral Eq.~\eq
         ref{eq:spinFGT}. The Van Vleck bump at the interband threshold is essentially unaffected by pairing at leading
         order in $\Delta/|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|$.}
    897 +			\label{fig:spinFGT-spectral}
    898 +		\end{figure}
    899 +		
    900 +		\clearpage

    496  	\label{sec:multiband}

    498 +	The last extension of Part~I changes not the control parameters but the Hilbert-space structure. We now lift the single-pocket assumption and ask ho
         w the geometric identities organize materials with several FS sheets.
    499 +	
    500  	\subsection{Disjoint-pocket form}
        вӢ®
    514  	
    507 -		Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} neglects inter-band pairing (exact for orbital-diagonal pairing). For orbitally mixed pairing, the formula is a
          decoupled-pocket baseline; deviations diagnose the inter-band structure. The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent
         of the orbital-diagonal assumption; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbital deriva
         tion is therefore required before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalized site/pocket bounds used there, to genuine inter-ban
         d pairing.
    515 +	Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} neglects inter-band pairing (exact for orbital-diagonal pairing). For orbitally mixed pairing, the formula is a dec
         oupled-pocket baseline; deviations diagnose the inter-band structure. The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of t
         he orbital-diagonal assumption; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbital derivation
          is therefore required before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalized site/pocket bounds used there, to genuine inter-band pa

    531  	
    524 -	\section*{Bridge: from identities to measurement}
    525 -	\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Bridge: from identities to measurement}
    532 +	\section*{Part II: From Identities to Measurement}
    533 +	\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Part II: From Identities to Measurement}
    534  	
    527 -	The remaining sections convert the identities of Part~I into experimental practice. The central geometric observation is that the strong-locking ide
         ntity Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified}, $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is a linear map from a symmetric traceful tensor ($\Pi$, $\mathrm{
         Tr}\,\Pi=1$) to a measurable symmetric tensor ($K_{\mu\nu}(0)$). A single-crystal Knight-shift rotation at $T=0$ therefore determines $\Pi$ directly, no
          fitting parameters. The eigenvalues of $\Pi$ live on a two-dimensional simplex, and each vertex, edge, and interior point corresponds to a distinct pai
         ring class. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} states this diagnostic. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} packages it, together with the finite-field, dynamical ($1/T
         _1$), site-resolved, and multi-band content of Part~I, into six experimental protocols. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} walks through K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$
          as a worked example.
    535 +	Part~I delivered four kinds of input: a scalar bound, a tensor geometry, controlled departures from the ideal strong-locking limit, and extensions t
         o dynamics and multiple pockets. Part~II turns those outputs into measurement logic. The central observation is that the strong-locking identity Eq.~\eq
         ref{eq:unified}, $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is a linear map from a symmetric traceful tensor ($\Pi$, $\mathrm{Tr}\,\Pi=1$)
         to a measurable symmetric tensor ($K_{\mu\nu}(0)$). A single-crystal Knight-shift rotation at $T=0$ therefore determines $\Pi$ directly, without fitting
          parameters. The simplex organizes the result geometrically; the protocol section then layers on the additional information from finite field, $1/T_1$,
         site resolution, and pocket decomposition. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} finally closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ as a worked example.
    536  	
    529 -	\section{Knight-shift ellipsoid and the simplex of pairing classes}
    537 +	\section[Core Diagnostic]{Core diagnostic: Knight-shift ellipsoid}
    538  	\label{sec:ellipsoid}
    539  	
    540 +	This section is the conceptual core of Part~II. It turns Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified} into a directly measurable geometric object and then classifies the
         allowed shapes before the protocol section operationalizes the construction.
    541 +	
    542  	\subsection{Knight-shift tensor and ellipsoid representation}
        вӢ®
    682  	
    673 -	This section collects six self-contained protocols that implement the identities of Part~I as concrete measurements. Table~\ref{tab:protocols} summa
         rizes what each protocol measures, its output, its assumptions, and the signature of failure. The protocols are ordered from the lightest (a single thre
         e-axis Knight shift) to the most demanding (multi-site multi-field program).
    683 +	This section converts the geometric dictionary of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} into a hierarchy of measurements. Protocol~B is the central one; the othe
         rs are either lighter entry points (Protocol~A) or targeted extensions and cross-checks (Protocols~C--F). Table~\ref{tab:protocols} summarizes what each
          protocol measures, its output, its assumptions, and the signature of failure. The protocols are ordered from the lightest (a single three-axis Knight s
         hift) to the most demanding (multi-site multi-field program).
    684  	
        вӢ®
    708  	
    699 -	\begin{enumerate}
    700 -		\item Measure $K_{\alpha}(T)$ for $\alpha=a,b,c$; extract $\chi_{\alpha\alpha}(T)$ via the hyperfine tensor.
    701 -		\item Form $r_{\alpha}\equiv\chi_{\alpha\alpha}(0)/\chi_{\alpha\alpha}(T_{c}^{+})$.
    702 -		\item Compute $R=\tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{\alpha}r_{\alpha}$.
    709 +		\begin{enumerate}
    710 +			\item Measure $K_{\alpha}(T)$ for $\alpha=a,b,c$; extract $\chi_{\alpha\alpha}(T)$ via the hyperfine tensor.
    711 +			\item Form $r_{\alpha}\equiv\chi_{\alpha\alpha}(0)/\chi_{\alpha\alpha}(T_{c}^{+})$.
    712 +			\item Compute $R=\tfrac{1}{3}\sum_{\alpha}r_{\alpha}$.
    713  			\item Compare against $R\le 2/3$ [Eq.~\eqref{eq:trace-bound}] on a 3D isotropic FS. Outside that setting, use Protocol~B rather than a three
         -axis scalar reduction.
    704 -		\item Compare the observed triple $(r_{a},r_{b},r_{c})$ against Table~\ref{tab:sat} to discriminate singlet vs OSP vs ESP and infer the $\mathbf
         {d}$-vector orientation.
    705 -	\end{enumerate}
    714 +			\item Compare the observed triple $(r_{a},r_{b},r_{c})$ against Table~\ref{tab:sat} to discriminate singlet vs OSP vs ESP and infer the $\ma
         thbf{d}$-vector orientation.
    715 +		\end{enumerate}
    716  	A violation $R>2/3$ signals breakdown of at least one assumption (multi-orbital physics, non-unitary triplet, first-order transition, mixed-parity p
         airing, FFLO, PDW, or strong correlations beyond mean field).
        вӢ®
    785  	
    776 -	\begin{enumerate}

    778 -		\item Verify the trace bound $\sum\chi_{\mu\mu}(0)=2\chi_{N}$.
    779 -		\item Apply pressure or composition tuning that shifts $w_{\parallel}$; track $\chi_{zz}(0)/\chi_{N}$ along the one-parameter family Eq.~\eqref{
         eq:a2cr3as3-par}.
    786 +		\begin{enumerate}
    787 +			\item From Protocols~A/B, extract $\chi_{xx}(0)-\chi_{zz}(0)=w_{\parallel}\chi_{N}$; this reads off the pocket weight directly.

    781 -	\end{enumerate}

    792  	In materials with significant inter-band pairing, deviations from Eq.~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} must be interpreted through a multi-orbital extension;
          Eq.~\eqref{eq:multiband-bound} should then be treated as part of the decoupled-pocket baseline unless it is rederived in the extended model.
        вӢ®
    796  	
    787 -		We walk through the protocols of Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} using the $^{75}$As Knight-shift and $1/T_{1}$ data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triple
         t2021} on single-crystal K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$. Three findings follow: (a) the observed ellipsoid sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the sim
         plex; (b) the band-resolved SOC-texture prediction Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} disagrees with the observation by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift
         units, favoring a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector mechanism within the orbital-diagonal baseline; (c) the observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ ca
         nnot come from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT budget and diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation.
    797 +	With the geometric dictionary and protocol hierarchy in place, we now close the loop on a concrete material. We walk through the protocols of Sec.~\
         ref{sec:protocols} using the $^{75}$As Knight-shift and $1/T_{1}$ data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021} on single-crystal K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}
         $. Three findings follow: (a) the observed ellipsoid sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the simplex; (b) the band-resolved SOC-texture predict
         ion Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} disagrees with the observation by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift units, favoring a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector me
         chanism within the orbital-diagonal baseline; (c) the observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT budg
         et and diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation.
    798  	
        вӢ®
    807  	with $\epsilon_{ab},\epsilon_{c}\ll 1$. The sixfold in-plane rotational symmetry of the Knight shift reported in Fig.~2C of Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021}
         forces the \emph{measured} $K_{\mu\nu}(T)$ to be axially symmetric about $\hat c$ with principal axes along the crystallographic $(a,b,c)$, independent
         of the microscopic hyperfine structure. The reduction from $K_{\mu\nu}$ to projection eigenvalues proceeds component by component:
    798 -	\begin{equation}
    799 -		\boxed{\;(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})^{\rm obs}=(0\pm\epsilon_{ab},\,0\pm\epsilon_{ab},\,1\pm\epsilon_{c}).\;}
    800 -		\label{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi}
    801 -	\end{equation}
    808 +		\begin{equation}
    809 +			\boxed{\;(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})^{\rm obs}=(0\pm\epsilon_{ab},\,0\pm\epsilon_{ab},\,1\pm\epsilon_{c}).\;}
    810 +			\label{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi}
    811 +		\end{equation}
    812  		The observed point sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} within experimental uncertainty, and $\sum\pi_{i}=1\pm O(
         \epsilon)$ saturates the universal constraint Eq.~\eqref{eq:trace-from-ellipsoid}. At the level of the measured ellipsoid, Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi} fi
         xes the effective locking texture to the $\hat c$ vertex of the simplex. Within the canonical single-band catalogue of Table~\ref{tab:ellipsoids}, this
         matches the \emph{OSP unitary spin-triplet with $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$} limit, or equivalently the quasi-1D $\hat n\parallel\hat c$ vertex. This re
         produces---via the ellipsoid diagnostic of Protocol~B, without invoking an $\mathbf{H}$-$T$ phase diagram---the principal conclusion of Ref.~\cite{Tripl
         et2021} at the level of the strong-locking single-band framework.
        вӢ®
    845  	Three pieces of evidence from Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} are organized under the framework:
    836 -	\begin{enumerate}
    837 -		\item Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c$ vertex of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex}, saturating the trace bound.
    838 -		\item Band-resolved SOC-texture prediction Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} with $w_{\gamma}=0.75$ off by $\sim 0.5$ in Knight-shift units; within t
         he orbital-diagonal baseline, the data favor a \emph{rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ on all three bands}.
    839 -		\item Suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from $\mathbf{q}=0$ budget; diagnosed as finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap f
         ormation.
    840 -	\end{enumerate}
    846 +		\begin{enumerate}
    847 +			\item Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c$ vertex of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex}, saturating the trace bound.
    848 +			\item Band-resolved SOC-texture prediction Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} with $w_{\gamma}=0.75$ off by $\sim 0.5$ in Knight-shift units; with
         in the orbital-diagonal baseline, the data favor a \emph{rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ on all three bands}.
    849 +			\item Suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from $\mathbf{q}=0$ budget; diagnosed as finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation g
         ap formation.
    850 +		\end{enumerate}
    851  		We distinguish two levels of conclusion. \emph{Robust experimental statement}: the Knight-shift tensor sits at the $\hat c$ vertex, and any acce
         ptable microscopic theory must reproduce that near-vertex ellipsoid. \emph{Baseline theoretical inference}: within the orbital-diagonal framework of Eq.
         ~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred}, the data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline. A definitive mic
         roscopic selection in the presence of strong inter-band pairing requires a multi-orbital BdG analysis---the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-su
         m-rule} survives that extension unchanged, but the specific anisotropic identities and the pocket/site bounds used in Part~II require rederivation.
        вӢ®
    857  	
    848 -	The Bogoliubov transformation's unitarity encodes a Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} from which the spin-sector sum rule of Ref.~
         \cite{pang2026} is one specialization. Part~I developed the hierarchy of $T=0$ NMR-relevant consequences: the trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le 2\chi
         _{N}$ on a 3D isotropic FS (Sec.~\ref{sec:isoT0}); the FS-shape-independent strong-locking anisotropic identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\n
         u}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}); the closed-form interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)$ at arbitrary SOC, validated to $10^{-3}$ against existing num
         erics (Sec.~\ref{sec:intermediate}); its strong-locking finite-field extension $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0,\mathbf{H})=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}(\mathb
         f{H})]$ with quadratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid (Sec.~\ref{sec:finiteH}); the spin-FGT sum rule tying Knight shift to integrated pair-break
         ing spectral weight (Sec.~\ref{sec:spinFGT}); and the multi-band decomposition with a structural prediction for the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family (Sec.
         ~\ref{sec:multiband}).
    858 +	The paper was organized as a progression from one exact identity to experimentally usable diagnostics. Part~I began with the Plancherel identity Eq.
         ~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} and extracted two static $T=0$ consequences in increasing generality: the isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le
          2\chi_{N}$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:isoT0}) and the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$
          (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}). These provide, respectively, the lightest scalar consistency test and the full geometric kernel used in the experimental manu
         al.
    859  	
    850 -	Part~II turned these identities into an experimental manual. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem and the two-dimen
         sional simplex of pairing classes, with a one-to-one correspondence between ellipsoid geometry and locking-vector texture (vertices=OSP/quasi-1D, edge-m
         idpoints=ESP/2D Rashba, barycenter=singlet strong SOC). Section~\ref{sec:protocols} collected six protocols of increasing sophistication: trace bound (A
         ), full-ellipsoid diagnostic (B), finite-field SOC-texture spectroscopy (C), $1/T_{1}$ cross-check (D), site-resolved consistency (E), and pocket decomp
         osition (F). The number of independent rotation angles required is set by crystal symmetry: two for uniaxial, three for orthorhombic, six for triclinic.
          Regimes outside strong locking, most notably the opening of a Bogoliubov Fermi surface, lie outside the closed-form reach of the framework and are flag
         ged as such.
    860 +	Part~I then treated controlled departures and extensions. Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} relaxed the infinite-locking assumption at $\mathbf{H}=0$ f
         or $s$-wave pairing and produced the closed-form interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)$, which contracts the strong-locking simplex radially. Section~\ref{sec:f
         initeH} restored finite Zeeman field within strong locking and showed quadratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid. Section~\ref{sec:spinFGT} supplie
         d the dynamical spin-FGT counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} extended the framework to multiple pockets, including the struc
         tural baseline for the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family.
    861  	
    852 -		Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} walked the protocols through the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021} on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$.
         The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ sits at the oblate-axial $\hat c$ vertex of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} and saturates the trace bound. Combi
         ned with the band-resolved DOS fractions reported there, the decoupled-pocket structural prediction of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband-A2Cr3As3} misses the obse
         rved vertex by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift units; within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the data favor a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat
          c$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture mechanism. The observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot originate from the $\m
         athbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT budget and is a direct fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation, consistent with the Curie-Weiss enhanc
         ement of $1/T_{1}T$ above $T_{c}$.
    853 -	
    854 -		The framework unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric principle: total matrix-element weight is the trace-squared of the pro
         be operator in the single-particle basis. Combined with band-structure input, it constrains different spin-triplet mechanisms at the same observed pairi
         ng symmetry. It invites systematic tests across the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family under pressure and composition tuning and across different NMR-active
          sites within a single compound. A definitive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter-band pairing requires a multi-orbital extension of t
         he specific anisotropic identities and protocol-level pocket/site bounds; the underlying Plancherel identity survives that extension unchanged.
    862 +	Part~II translated these outputs into measurement logic. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the ellipsoid theorem and pairing simplex; Sec.~\ref{sec
         :protocols} organized six protocols ordered by experimental cost and theoretical input; Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closed the loop on the $^{75}$As data of
          Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ fixes the Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c$ vertex and sat
         urates the trace bound. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the decoupled-pocket SOC-texture prediction misses the data by $\sim 0.5$, so the data fav
         or a common-$\mathbf{d}$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline, while the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ diagnoses fi
         nite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation. The framework therefore unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric principle and m
         akes explicit which parts of the inference are robust experimentally and which remain baseline-theoretical pending a genuine multi-orbital extension.
    863

вҖў IвҖҷve read the current v11 pass. The remaining flow issues are mostly structural rather than algebraic: the abstract is still overloaded, the multiband and
  protocol sections read slightly more like a catalogue than a progression, and the K$_2$Cr$_3$As$_3$ example can be tied more explicitly to Protocols B/F/D.

  Plan for the next revision:

  1. Tighten the abstract and introduction so the paper has one visible spine: exact identity вҶ’ static consequences вҶ’ controlled departures/extensions вҶ’
     measurement manual вҶ’ worked example.
  2. Smooth the Part I handoff near intermediate-SOC, spin-FGT, and multiband so each section states exactly what it adds to the main thread.
  3. Reshape Part II so the ellipsoid section feels like the central theorem, the protocol section like an ordered deployment guide, and the K$_2$Cr$_3$As$_3$
     section like a direct application of specific protocols.
  4. Move or trim anything that feels explanatory but nonessential to the main line if it improves readability without weakening the argument.
  5. Save as SumRule-v12.tex and recompile.

     38  	
     39 -	\begin{abstract}
     40 -		We present a unified sum-rule framework for the NMR response of single-band noncentrosymmetric superconductors in arbitrary Zeeman field and Ras
         hba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC), organized in two parts. \emph{Part~I} (theory) establishes the framework. A Plancherel identity $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+
         \sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr}_{s}(O^{2})$, valid for every Hermitian single-particle operator $O$, underlies (i) a trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le
         \tfrac{2}{3}\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T_{c})=2\chi_{N}$ on a 3D isotropic Fermi surface; (ii) a strong-locking anisotropic identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\
         delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$ with the locking projector $\Pi_{\mu\nu}=\langle\hat n_{\mu}\hat n_{\nu}\rangle_{\rm FS}$; (iii) a closed-form $s$-wave in
         terpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)=1-\sinh^{-1}\lambda/[\lambda\sqrt{1+\lambda^{2}}]$ at arbitrary SOC, validated against existing numerics to $10^{-3}$; (iv)
         a finite-field extension $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0,\mathbf{H})=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}(\mathbf{H})]$ stable to $O(h^{2}/\lambda^{2})$ against small
          applied fields; (v) a spin analogue of the Ferrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule tying the Knight-shift deficit to integrated pair-breaking spectral weight
         ; and (vi) the multi-band decomposition. \emph{Part~II} (manual) converts these identities into experimental practice: the Knight-shift ellipsoid theore
         m and the two-dimensional simplex of pairing classes (one-to-one map from ellipsoid geometry to locking texture); a hyperfine-invariant site-resolved bo
         und as an internal consistency check; and six protocols covering trace-bound tests, full-ellipsoid diagnostics, finite-field SOC-texture spectroscopy, $
         1/T_{1}$ cross-checks, site-resolved tests, and pocket decomposition. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, the framework places
          the observed projection at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$, saturates the trace bound, and, within the orbital-diagonal baseline, favors a rigid comm
         on $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture one, while identifying the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$
          as a fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation gap formation. Regimes outside strong locking, most notably Bogoliubov-Fermi-sur
         face formation, are explicitly outside the closed-form reach of the framework.
     41 -	\end{abstract}
     39 +		\begin{abstract}
     40 +			We present a two-part framework for the NMR response of single-band noncentrosymmetric superconductors in Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-
         orbit coupling (SOC). \emph{Part~I} starts from a Plancherel identity, $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+\sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr}_{s}(O^{2})$, valid for every H
         ermitian single-particle operator $O$. From it we derive an isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le\tfrac{2}{3}\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T_{c})=2\chi_
         {N}$, the strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$, a closed-form $s$-wave interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)
         =1-\sinh^{-1}\lambda/[\lambda\sqrt{1+\lambda^{2}}]$ at arbitrary SOC validated against existing numerics, its finite-field strong-locking extension stab
         le to $O(h^{2}/\lambda^{2})$ against small applied fields, a spin analogue of the Ferrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule, and a decoupled multiband baseline.
          \emph{Part~II} turns these outputs into measurement logic: the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem and simplex of pairing classes, a hyperfine-invariant sit
         e-resolved bound, and six protocols ranging from the trace-bound test to pocket decomposition. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{
         3}$, the observed point lies at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$, saturates the trace bound, disfavors the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline in f
         avor of a rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ interpretation within the orbital-diagonal setting, and identifies the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parall
         el\hat c$ as a fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation gap formation. Regimes outside strong locking, most notably Bogoliubov-
         Fermi-surface formation, remain outside the closed-form reach of the framework.
     41 +		\end{abstract}

     50 -	Reference~\cite{pang2026} developed a self-consistent single-band Bogoliubov--de Gennes (BdG) theory for the static uniform spin susceptibility $\ch
         i_{\mu\nu}(T)$ of a superconductor under simultaneous Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC)~\cite{Rashiba1960,Dresselhaus}. A key techn
         ical ingredient there is a matrix-element sum rule (Eq.~(14) of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}) on the Bogoliubov transformation. In this paper we recast that sum
          rule as one member of a broader Plancherel family and use that reorganization to make the downstream logic explicit. Once the exact identity is establi
         shed, the remainder of the paper separates naturally into static $T=0$ consequences, controlled departures from the ideal strong-locking limit, and expe
         rimentally relevant extensions to dynamics and multiple bands.
     50 +		Reference~\cite{pang2026} developed a self-consistent single-band Bogoliubov--de Gennes (BdG) theory for the static uniform spin susceptibility
         $\chi_{\mu\nu}(T)$ of a superconductor under simultaneous Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling (SOC)~\cite{Rashiba1960,Dresselhaus}. A key t
         echnical ingredient there is a matrix-element sum rule (Eq.~(14) of Ref.~\cite{pang2026}) on the Bogoliubov transformation. In this paper we elevate tha
         t relation to one member of a broader Plancherel family and organize the manuscript around its consequences. The logical spine is simple: exact identity
          $\rightarrow$ static $T=0$ consequences $\rightarrow$ controlled departures and extensions $\rightarrow$ experimental manual.

         7$.

         roduced by pair formation must reappear as additional spectral weight at finite frequency in $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)$. We state this identit
         y, trace its origin to the generalized sum rule of Sec.~\ref{sec:generalized}, and discuss its relation to the spin-lattice relaxation rate $1/T_{1}$.
    417 +		The preceding sections fixed the static geometry of $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)$. We now turn to its dynamical counterpart: whatever Knight-shift deficit

         lies the only additional input needed for the $1/T_{1}$ cross-check of Part~II: a budget relation between the static ellipsoid and dynamical spectral-we
         ight transfer.

    502 +		\section[Structural Extension]{Structural extension: decoupled multiband baseline}
    503 +		\label{sec:multiband}
    504 +		
    505 +		The last extension of Part~I changes not the control parameters but the Hilbert-space structure. Because the fully general multi-orbital problem
          is broader than the present paper, we isolate the piece that Part~II can use directly: the decoupled-pocket baseline in which each FS sheet contributes
          additively and deviations can be read as signatures of inter-band structure.

    513 -	\subsection{Inter-band mixing}
    514 -	
    515 -	Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} neglects inter-band pairing (exact for orbital-diagonal pairing). For orbitally mixed pairing, the formula is a dec
         oupled-pocket baseline; deviations diagnose the inter-band structure. The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of t
         he orbital-diagonal assumption; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbital derivation
          is therefore required before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalized site/pocket bounds used there, to genuine inter-band pa
         iring.
    520 +		\subsection{Scope: inter-band pairing as deviation from baseline}
    521 +		
    522 +		Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} is exact for orbital-diagonal pairing. For orbitally mixed pairing it becomes a baseline rather than a theorem:
          deviations diagnose the inter-band structure. The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of the orbital-diagonal ass
         umption; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbital derivation is therefore required
         before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalized site/pocket bounds used there, to genuine inter-band pairing.

         o dynamics and multiple pockets. Part~II turns those outputs into measurement logic. The central observation is that the strong-locking identity Eq.~\eq
         ref{eq:unified}, $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is a linear map from a symmetric traceful tensor ($\Pi$, $\mathrm{Tr}\,\Pi=1$)
         to a measurable symmetric tensor ($K_{\mu\nu}(0)$). A single-crystal Knight-shift rotation at $T=0$ therefore determines $\Pi$ directly, without fitting
          parameters. The simplex organizes the result geometrically; the protocol section then layers on the additional information from finite field, $1/T_1$,
         site resolution, and pocket decomposition. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} finally closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ as a worked example.
    541 +		Part~I produced four kinds of input: a scalar bound, a tensor geometry, controlled departures from the ideal strong-locking limit, and structura

         :unified}, $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is read directly as a map from the measurable Knight-shift tensor to the projection t
         ensor $\Pi$; this is the ellipsoid theorem of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}. Next, Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the remaining ingredients as successive

    687 +		
    688 +		\section[Experimental Manual]{Experimental manual: six protocols}
    689 +		\label{sec:protocols}
    690 +		
    691 +		This section converts the geometric dictionary of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} into an ordered deployment guide. If only three orthogonal Knight shi
         fts are available, start with Protocol~A. If a full single-crystal rotation is available, Protocol~B becomes the central measurement because it determin
         es the ellipsoid directly. Protocols~C--F then add one extra layer each: field dependence, dynamical cross-checks, site consistency, and pocket decompos
         ition. Table~\ref{tab:protocols} summarizes what each protocol measures, its output, its assumptions, and the signature of failure.
    692  	
    679 -	\section{Experimental protocols}
    680 -	\label{sec:protocols}
    681 -	
    682 -	This section converts the geometric dictionary of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} into a hierarchy of measurements. Protocol~B is the central one; the othe
         rs are either lighter entry points (Protocol~A) or targeted extensions and cross-checks (Protocols~C--F). Table~\ref{tab:protocols} summarizes what each
          protocol measures, its output, its assumptions, and the signature of failure. The protocols are ordered from the lightest (a single three-axis Knight s
         hift) to the most demanding (multi-site multi-field program).

         $. Three findings follow: (a) the observed ellipsoid sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the simplex; (b) the band-resolved SOC-texture predict
         ion Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} disagrees with the observation by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift units, favoring a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector me
         chanism within the orbital-diagonal baseline; (c) the observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT budg
         et and diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation.

    841 -	\subsection{Verdict and caveats}
    842 -	\label{sec:K2Cr3As3-verdict}
    843 -	
    844 -	Three pieces of evidence from Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} are organized under the framework:
    850 +		\subsection{Interpretation and scope}
    851 +		\label{sec:K2Cr3As3-verdict}
    852 +		
    853 +		Putting the three protocols together separates what is fixed directly by experiment from what remains a baseline theoretical inference. Three pi
         eces of evidence from Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} are organized under the framework:
    854  		\begin{enumerate}
        вӢ®
    865  	
    857 -	The paper was organized as a progression from one exact identity to experimentally usable diagnostics. Part~I began with the Plancherel identity Eq.
         ~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} and extracted two static $T=0$ consequences in increasing generality: the isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le
          2\chi_{N}$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:isoT0}) and the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$
          (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}). These provide, respectively, the lightest scalar consistency test and the full geometric kernel used in the experimental manu
         al.
    858 -	
    859 -	Part~I then treated controlled departures and extensions. Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} relaxed the infinite-locking assumption at $\mathbf{H}=0$ f
         or $s$-wave pairing and produced the closed-form interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)$, which contracts the strong-locking simplex radially. Section~\ref{sec:f
         initeH} restored finite Zeeman field within strong locking and showed quadratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid. Section~\ref{sec:spinFGT} supplie
         d the dynamical spin-FGT counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} extended the framework to multiple pockets, including the struc
         tural baseline for the A$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ family.
    860 -	
    861 -	Part~II translated these outputs into measurement logic. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the ellipsoid theorem and pairing simplex; Sec.~\ref{sec
         :protocols} organized six protocols ordered by experimental cost and theoretical input; Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closed the loop on the $^{75}$As data of
          Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ fixes the Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c$ vertex and sat
         urates the trace bound. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the decoupled-pocket SOC-texture prediction misses the data by $\sim 0.5$, so the data fav
         or a common-$\mathbf{d}$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline, while the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ diagnoses fi
         nite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation. The framework therefore unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric principle and m
         akes explicit which parts of the inference are robust experimentally and which remain baseline-theoretical pending a genuine multi-orbital extension.
    866 +		The paper was organized as a progression from one exact identity to experimentally usable diagnostics. Part~I began with the Plancherel identity
          Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} and extracted two static $T=0$ consequences in increasing generality: the isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0
         )\le 2\chi_{N}$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:isoT0}) and the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\n
         u}]$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}). These provide, respectively, the lightest scalar consistency test and the full geometric kernel that Part~II reads out ex
         perimentally.
    867 +		
    868 +		Part~I then treated controlled departures and extensions. Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} relaxed the infinite-locking assumption at $\mathbf{H}=
         0$ for $s$-wave pairing and produced the closed-form interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)$, which contracts the strong-locking simplex radially. Section~\ref{s
         ec:finiteH} restored finite Zeeman field within strong locking and showed quadratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid. Section~\ref{sec:spinFGT} sup
         plied the dynamical spin-FGT counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} isolated the decoupled-pocket multiband baseline used later
          as an experimental comparison point.
    869 +		
    870 +		Part~II then used those outputs in measurement order. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the ellipsoid theorem and pairing simplex; Sec.~\ref{se
         c:protocols} organized six protocols from the lightest scalar check to the multiband baseline test; Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} applied that manual to the $
         ^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ fixes the Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c
         $ vertex and saturates the trace bound. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the decoupled-pocket SOC-texture prediction misses the data by $\sim 0.5$,
          so the data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline, while the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat
          c$ diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation. The framework therefore unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric
          principle and makes explicit which parts of the inference are robust experimentally and which remain baseline-theoretical pending a genuine multi-orbit
         al extension.

    51  	
    52 -	The paper is organized in two parts. \emph{Part~I} (Secs.~\ref{sec:setup}--\ref{sec:multiband}) develops the theory in dependency order. Section~\ref
        {sec:setup} fixes notation. Section~\ref{sec:generalized} establishes the exact generalized sum rule as a Plancherel identity. Section~\ref{sec:isoT0} de
        rives the simplest scalar consequence, an isotropic-FS trace bound. Section~\ref{sec:anisoT0} then trades isotropy for strong locking and yields the tens
        or identity that becomes the backbone of Part~II. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{sec:finiteH} relax one assumption at a time: the former keeps
        $\mathbf{H}=0$ but allows arbitrary SOC strength for $s$-wave pairing, while the latter restores finite Zeeman field but returns to strong locking. Secti
        on~\ref{sec:spinFGT} gives the dynamical counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} gives the structural extension to multiple pocke
        ts.

        } derives the simplest scalar consequence, an isotropic-FS trace bound. Section~\ref{sec:anisoT0} then trades isotropy for strong locking and yields the

         the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem: rotating the applied field on a single crystal at $T=0$ produces a quadric surface whose principal values, together
        with the trace constraint of Part~I, diagnose the pairing class via a two-dimensional simplex. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the theory into six m
        easurement protocols ordered by experimental cost and theoretical input. Their dependency is simple: Protocol~A uses only the isotropic trace bound; Prot

        ections of Part~I. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ using the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021
        }. Section~\ref{sec:summary} summarizes.

         m}\,\chi/\omega$ is preserved.

    447 +		\subsection{Physical content: spectral-weight transfer}
    448 +		
    449 +		The content of Eq.~\eqref{eq:spinFGT} is simple. In the normal state, the Pauli component sits at $\omega\to 0$ (Landau-broadened at finite $T$)
         , with additional Van Vleck weight at the SOC-induced interband threshold. In the superconducting state, the $\omega\to 0$ weight is reduced by the fact
         or $1-\pi_{\mu}$, and the displaced weight reappears above $2\Delta_{\min}$ as pair-breaking absorption. The total integrated $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi/\omega$
          is preserved. A schematic is deferred to Appendix~\ref{app:spinFGT-support} so that the main text can keep the dynamical argument on the same footing a
         s the static derivation.
    450  	
        вӢ®
    868  	\par
    892 -	
    893 -	\subsection{Heuristic extension to unitary triplet with anisotropic gap}
    894 -	
    895 -	Replacing $\Delta\to|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|$ in the $\xi$-integration suggests the ansatz $F_{\rm t}(\lambda_{\mathbf{k}})\approx F_{s}(|\mathbf{g}
         _{\mathbf{k}}|/|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|)$, with $F_{\rm t}\to 1$ at nodes. This captures the gap-magnitude dependence exactly when the pairing amplitude
          is spatially constant across the FS, but misses corrections from the $\hat d$-vector angular structure and from intra-band triplet channels. A rigorous
          derivation is beyond the present scope.

    871 +		
    872 +		Replacing $\Delta\to|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|$ in the $\xi$-integration suggests the ansatz $F_{\rm t}(\lambda_{\mathbf{k}})\approx F_{s}(|\mathb
         f{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|/|\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})|)$, with $F_{\rm t}\to 1$ at nodes. This captures the gap-magnitude dependence exactly when the pairing ampli
         tude is spatially constant across the FS, but misses corrections from the $\hat d$-vector angular structure and from intra-band triplet channels. A rigo
         rous derivation is beyond the present scope.
