вҖў Edited SumRule-v14.tex (+4 -4)
    39  		\begin{abstract}
    40 -			We present a two-part framework for the NMR response of single-band noncentrosymmetric superconductors in Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-o
        rbit coupling (SOC). \emph{Part~I} starts from a Plancherel identity, $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+\sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr}_{s}(O^{2})$, valid for every Her
        mitian single-particle operator $O$. From it we derive an isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le\tfrac{2}{3}\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T_{c})=2\chi_{N}
        $, the strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$, a closed-form $s$-wave interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)=1-\
        sinh^{-1}\lambda/[\lambda\sqrt{1+\lambda^{2}}]$ at arbitrary SOC validated against existing numerics, its finite-field strong-locking extension stable to
         $O(h^{2}/\lambda^{2})$ against small applied fields, a spin analogue of the Ferrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule, and a decoupled multiband baseline. \emph
        {Part~II} turns these outputs into measurement logic: the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem and simplex of pairing classes, a hyperfine-invariant site-resol
        ved bound, and six protocols ranging from the trace-bound test to pocket decomposition. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, the
         observed point lies at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$, saturates the trace bound, disfavors the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline in favor of a
         rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ interpretation within the orbital-diagonal setting, and identifies the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$
        as a fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation gap formation. Regimes outside strong locking, most notably Bogoliubov-Fermi-surfa
        ce formation, remain outside the closed-form reach of the framework.
    40 +			We present a unified framework for the NMR response of single-band noncentrosymmetric superconductors in Zeeman field and Rashba-type spin-or
        bit coupling (SOC). Starting from a Plancherel identity, $\sum|M_{ph,O}|^{2}+\sum|M_{pp,O}|^{2}=\mathrm{Tr}_{s}(O^{2})$, valid for every Hermitian single
        -particle operator $O$, we derive an isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le\tfrac{2}{3}\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T_{c})=2\chi_{N}$, the strong-locking
         tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$, a parity-mixed extension showing that fully gapped helicity-diagonal singlet-
        triplet mixtures retain the same $T=0$ projector geometry while differing in finite-$T$ recovery, a closed-form $s$-wave interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)=1-
        \sinh^{-1}\lambda/[\lambda\sqrt{1+\lambda^{2}}]$ at arbitrary SOC validated against existing numerics, its finite-field strong-locking extension stable t
        o $O(h^{2}/\lambda^{2})$ against small applied fields, a spin analogue of the Ferrell--Glover--Tinkham sum rule, and a decoupled multiband baseline. We t
        hen turn these outputs into measurement logic: the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem and simplex of locking classes, a hyperfine-invariant site-resolved bou
        nd, and six protocols ranging from the trace-bound test to pocket decomposition. Applied to the $^{75}$As NMR data on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, the observ
        ed point lies at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$, saturates the trace bound, fixes a $\hat c$-axis locking geometry, disfavors the independent-pocket S
        OC-texture baseline, and identifies the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ as a fingerprint of finite-$\mathbf{q}$ ferromagnetic spin-fluctuation ga
        p formation. Regimes outside strong locking, most notably Bogoliubov-Fermi-surface formation, remain outside the closed-form reach of the framework.
    41  		\end{abstract}
       вӢ®
    51  	
    52 -		The paper is organized in two parts. \emph{Part~I} (Secs.~\ref{sec:setup}--\ref{sec:multiband}) develops the theory in dependency order. Section~
        \ref{sec:setup} fixes notation. Section~\ref{sec:generalized} establishes the exact generalized sum rule as a Plancherel identity. Section~\ref{sec:isoT0
        } derives the simplest scalar consequence, an isotropic-FS trace bound. Section~\ref{sec:anisoT0} then trades isotropy for strong locking and yields the
        tensor identity that becomes the backbone of Part~II. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{sec:finiteH} relax one assumption at a time: the former ke
        eps $\mathbf{H}=0$ but allows arbitrary SOC strength for $s$-wave pairing, while the latter restores finite Zeeman field but returns to strong locking. S
        ection~\ref{sec:spinFGT} gives the dynamical counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} isolates the decoupled multiband baseline us
        ed later in the experimental manual.
    53 -	
    54 -		\emph{Part~II} (Secs.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}--\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}) converts those outputs into an experimental manual. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} st
        ates the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem: rotating the applied field on a single crystal at $T=0$ produces a quadric surface whose principal values, toget
        her with the trace constraint of Part~I, diagnose the pairing class via a two-dimensional simplex. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the theory into s
        ix measurement protocols ordered by experimental cost and theoretical input. Their dependency is simple: Protocol~A uses only the isotropic trace bound;
        Protocol~B uses the strong-locking tensor identity; Protocols~C--F add the finite-field, dynamical, site-resolved, and multiband-baseline ingredients fro
        m the later sections of Part~I. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} closes the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ using the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cit
        e{Triplet2021}. Section~\ref{sec:summary} summarizes.
    52 +		The paper follows the dependency structure of the results. Section~\ref{sec:setup} fixes notation. Section~\ref{sec:generalized} establishes the
        exact generalized sum rule as a Plancherel identity. Sections~\ref{sec:isoT0} and~\ref{sec:anisoT0} extract the two static $T=0$ consequences used most d
        irectly in experiment: first an isotropic-FS trace bound, then the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity. Sections~\ref{sec:paritymix-struc
        ture} and~\ref{sec:paritymix-consequences} place parity-mixed singlet-triplet states in the same language and show that, in the fully gapped helicity-dia
        gonal limit, the $T=0$ projector survives while the admixture is pushed primarily into the finite-$T$ recovery. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{
        sec:finiteH} then relax one assumption at a time: the former keeps $\mathbf{H}=0$ but allows arbitrary SOC strength for $s$-wave pairing, while the latte
        r restores finite Zeeman field but returns to strong locking. Section~\ref{sec:spinFGT} gives the dynamical counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\
        ref{sec:multiband} isolates the decoupled multiband baseline used later in the measurement analysis.
    53 +
    54 +		The later sections turn those outputs into an experimental manual. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} states the Knight-shift ellipsoid theorem: rotatin
        g the applied field on a single crystal at $T=0$ produces a quadric surface whose principal values, together with the trace constraint, diagnose the lock
        ing class via a two-dimensional simplex. Section~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the theory into six measurement protocols ordered by experimental cost and
        theoretical input. Their dependency is simple: Protocol~A uses only the isotropic trace bound; Protocol~B uses the strong-locking tensor identity; Protoc
        ols~C--F add the finite-field, dynamical, site-resolved, and multiband-baseline ingredients from the later theory sections. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} cl
        oses the loop on K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ using the $^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. Section~\ref{sec:summary} summarizes.

    216  	
    217 -	Inequality~\eqref{eq:trace-bound} is pairing-symmetry-independent, SOC-strength-independent, and Zeeman-field-independent within the single-band uni
         tary BdG framework whose transition at the measurement field is continuous~\cite{Maki1964,Clogston1962,Chandrasekhar62}. Violation signals breakdown of
         at least one of those assumptions (multi-orbital physics, non-unitary triplet, first-order transition, mixed-parity pairing, FFLO~\cite{FF_state,LO_stat
         e}, pair-density-wave order~\cite{PDW}, or strong correlations beyond mean field).
    217 +	Inequality~\eqref{eq:trace-bound} is pairing-symmetry-independent, SOC-strength-independent, and Zeeman-field-independent within the single-band uni
         tary BdG framework whose transition at the measurement field is continuous~\cite{Maki1964,Clogston1962,Chandrasekhar62}. Violation signals breakdown of
         at least one of those assumptions (multi-orbital physics, non-unitary triplet, first-order transition, mixed-parity pairing outside the helicity-diagona
         l fully gapped limit, FFLO~\cite{FF_state,LO_state}, pair-density-wave order~\cite{PDW}, or strong correlations beyond mean field).

         rical-FS assumption at the price of strong locking and yields the tensor identity that later becomes the ellipsoid diagnostic of Part~II.

         rical-FS assumption at the price of strong locking and yields the tensor identity that later becomes the ellipsoid diagnostic of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid
         }.

    293 -	with $\hat n$ the dominant locking vector ($\hat g_{\mathbf{k}}$, $\hat d_{\mathbf{k}}$, or normalized $\mu_{B}\mathbf{H}+\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}$ i
         n the respective limits). This single identity underlies every geometric diagnostic in Part~II.
    294 -	
    295 -	Equation~\eqref{eq:unified} is the main geometric theorem of the paper. The next two sections ask how it changes when one relaxes, one at a time, th
         e two assumptions used above: infinite locking magnitude and zero applied field.
    293 +	with $\hat n$ the dominant locking vector ($\hat g_{\mathbf{k}}$, $\hat d_{\mathbf{k}}$, or normalized $\mu_{B}\mathbf{H}+\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}$ i
         n the respective limits). This single identity underlies every geometric diagnostic developed below.
    294 +
    295 +	Equation~\eqref{eq:unified} is the main geometric theorem of the paper. Before relaxing its assumptions, it is useful to place parity-mixed singlet-
         triplet states in the same language. The next two sections show that the fully gapped helicity-diagonal limit preserves the $T=0$ projector geometry, an
         d that parity admixture primarily reorganizes the finite-$T$ recovery. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{sec:finiteH} then relax, one at a time,
         the infinite-locking and zero-field assumptions.

вҖў Edited SumRule-v14.tex (+56 -1)
    295  	Equation~\eqref{eq:unified} is the main geometric theorem of the paper. Before relaxing its assumptions, it is useful to place parity-mixed singlet-
         triplet states in the same language. The next two sections show that the fully gapped helicity-diagonal limit preserves the $T=0$ projector geometry, an
         d that parity admixture primarily reorganizes the finite-$T$ recovery. Sections~\ref{sec:intermediate} and~\ref{sec:finiteH} then relax, one at a time,
         the infinite-locking and zero-field assumptions.
    296 -	
    296 +
    297 +	\section[Parity-Mixed I]{Parity-mixed pairing I: helicity-basis structure}
    298 +	\label{sec:paritymix-structure}
    299 +
    300 +		Because inversion symmetry is absent, parity is not a good quantum number and a generic superconducting state mixes even spin-singlet and odd sp
         in-triplet components~\cite{Frigeri2004,Samokhin2007,Edelstein2008,bauer2012non,Smidman17}. In the notation of Sec.~\ref{sec:setup},
    301 +	\begin{equation}
    302 +		\Delta(\mathbf{k})=i[\psi(\mathbf{k})\sigma_{0}+\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})\cdot\hat\sigma]\sigma_{y},
    303 +		\label{eq:paritymix-gap}
    304 +	\end{equation}
    305 +	with $\psi(-\mathbf{k})=\psi(\mathbf{k})$ and $\mathbf{d}(-\mathbf{k})=-\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})$. At $\mathbf{H}=0$, the natural spin-quantization ax
         is is the helicity direction $\hat g_{\mathbf{k}}=\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}/|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|$. Decompose the triplet part into longitudinal and t
         ransverse pieces,
    306 +	\begin{equation}
    307 +		\mathbf{d}(\mathbf{k})=d_{\parallel}(\mathbf{k})\hat g_{\mathbf{k}}+\mathbf{d}_{\perp}(\mathbf{k}),\qquad d_{\parallel}(\mathbf{k})=\mathbf{d}(\
         mathbf{k})\cdot\hat g_{\mathbf{k}}.
    308 +		\label{eq:paritymix-dsplit}
    309 +	\end{equation}
    310 +	In the helicity basis of the normal-state Hamiltonian, the pairing matrix then takes the schematic form
    311 +	\begin{equation}
    312 +		\tilde\Delta(\mathbf{k})=
    313 +		\begin{pmatrix}
    314 +			\Delta_{+}(\mathbf{k}) & \Delta_{\perp}(\mathbf{k})\\
    315 +			-\Delta_{\perp}(\mathbf{k}) & \Delta_{-}(\mathbf{k})
    316 +		\end{pmatrix},
    317 +		\qquad
    318 +		\Delta_{\pm}(\mathbf{k})=\psi(\mathbf{k})\pm d_{\parallel}(\mathbf{k}),
    319 +		\label{eq:paritymix-helicity-gap}
    320 +	\end{equation}
    321 +	where $\Delta_{\perp}(\mathbf{k})\propto\mathbf{d}_{\perp}(\mathbf{k})$ mixes the two helicity sheets. The geometrically simplest regime is therefor
         e the \emph{helicity-diagonal} limit $\Delta_{\perp}=0$, realized exactly when $\mathbf{d}\parallel\mathbf{g}$ and approximately whenever the antisymmet
         ric SOC splitting is large compared with transverse inter-helicity pairing. In that limit, parity mixing changes the gap amplitudes on the $\pm$ sheets
         but not the underlying locking texture $\hat n_{\mathbf{k}}=\hat g_{\mathbf{k}}$.
    322 +
    323 +	\section[Parity-Mixed II]{Parity-mixed pairing II: what survives and what changes}
    324 +	\label{sec:paritymix-consequences}
    325 +
    326 +		The geometric point is that the Knight-shift tensor is controlled first by the locking texture and only second by the parity label. In the fully
          gapped helicity-diagonal limit, the susceptibility can be written as
    327 +	\begin{equation}
    328 +		\frac{\chi_{\mu\nu}(T)}{\chi_{N}}
    329 +		=
    330 +		\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}
    331 +		+
    332 +		\sum_{\lambda=\pm}w_{\lambda}
    333 +		\left\langle
    334 +			Y_{\lambda}(\mathbf{k},T)\hat n_{\mu}(\mathbf{k})\hat n_{\nu}(\mathbf{k})
    335 +		\right\rangle_{\lambda},
    336 +		\label{eq:paritymix-chiT}
    337 +	\end{equation}
    338 +		where $\langle\cdots\rangle_{\lambda}$ denotes a Fermi-surface average over the $\lambda$-helicity sheet, $w_{\lambda}=N_{\lambda}(0)/N(0)$ with
          $\sum_{\lambda}w_{\lambda}=1$, $Y_{\lambda}$ is the usual Yosida factor built from $|\Delta_{\lambda}(\mathbf{k})|$, and $\Pi_{\mu\nu}\equiv\sum_{\lamb
         da}w_{\lambda}\langle\hat n_{\mu}\hat n_{\nu}\rangle_{\lambda}$ is the helicity-resolved generalization of Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pidef}. For a fully gapped stat
         e, $Y_{\lambda}\to 0$ as $T\to 0$, so
    339 +	\begin{equation}
    340 +		\boxed{\;\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}].\;}
    341 +		\label{eq:paritymix-T0}
    342 +	\end{equation}
    343 +	A fully gapped helicity-diagonal parity mixture therefore does \emph{not} generate a new $T=0$ simplex point: it shares the same projector geometry
         as the corresponding locking texture. What changes is the finite-$T$ recovery, because the two helicity sheets generally carry different gaps $\Delta_{\
         pm}$. Taking the trace of Eq.~\eqref{eq:paritymix-chiT} gives
    344 +	\begin{equation}
    345 +		\frac{\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(T)}{\chi_{N}}
    346 +		=
    347 +		2+\sum_{\lambda=\pm}w_{\lambda}\langle Y_{\lambda}(\mathbf{k},T)\rangle_{\lambda},
    348 +		\label{eq:paritymix-trace}
    349 +	\end{equation}
    350 +	using $\mathrm{Tr}\,\Pi=1$. Unequal $\Delta_{\pm}$ therefore produce a broadened or two-scale return of the Knight shift with temperature even when
         the $T=0$ ellipsoid is unchanged. Once $\Delta_{\perp}$ is sizable, one helicity sheet is weakly paired or gapless, or inter-band pairing dominates, Eq.
         ~\eqref{eq:paritymix-T0} should be treated as a baseline rather than a theorem.
    351 +

    466  		\end{equation}
    467 -		valid both at low field ($\mu_B H\ll gk_F$, covered by Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi-H-weak}) and high field ($\mu_B H\gg gk_F$, Eq.~\eqref{eq:strongZeeman})
         , but not in the crossover window around $H^{\ast}$. Outside the strong-locking regime, no closed form is known. We verified numerically that naive subs
         titutions such as $\Delta\to\sqrt{\Delta^2-(\mu_B H)^2}$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:chi-explicit} do not reproduce the self-consistent BdG result, departing by $O
         (1)$ in $\chi/\chi_N$ across the bulk of the $(g,H)$ phase diagram; the problem is structural (the BdG fails to block-diagonalize) rather than a matter
         of substitution. Protocol~C in Part~II is built precisely around this regime separation: simple ellipsoid deformation away from the crossover, full nume
         rics near $H^{\ast}$.
    467 +		valid both at low field ($\mu_B H\ll gk_F$, covered by Eq.~\eqref{eq:Pi-H-weak}) and high field ($\mu_B H\gg gk_F$, Eq.~\eqref{eq:strongZeeman})
         , but not in the crossover window around $H^{\ast}$. Outside the strong-locking regime, no closed form is known. We verified numerically that naive subs
         titutions such as $\Delta\to\sqrt{\Delta^2-(\mu_B H)^2}$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:chi-explicit} do not reproduce the self-consistent BdG result, departing by $O
         (1)$ in $\chi/\chi_N$ across the bulk of the $(g,H)$ phase diagram; the problem is structural (the BdG fails to block-diagonalize) rather than a matter
         of substitution. Protocol~C below is built precisely around this regime separation: simple ellipsoid deformation away from the crossover, full numerics
         near $H^{\ast}$.

    471  	
    472 -		The preceding sections fixed the static geometry of $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)$. We now turn to its dynamical counterpart: whatever Knight-shift deficit
         is produced by pair formation must reappear as additional spectral weight at finite frequency in $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)$. This section supp
         lies the only additional input needed for the $1/T_{1}$ cross-check of Part~II: a budget relation between the static ellipsoid and dynamical spectral-we
         ight transfer.
    472 +		The preceding sections fixed the static geometry of $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)$. We now turn to its dynamical counterpart: whatever Knight-shift deficit
         is produced by pair formation must reappear as additional spectral weight at finite frequency in $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)$. This section supp
         lies the only additional input needed for the $1/T_{1}$ cross-check developed later: a budget relation between the static ellipsoid and dynamical spectr
         al-weight transfer.
    473  	
        вӢ®
    536  		
    537 -		The last extension of Part~I changes not the control parameters but the Hilbert-space structure. Because the fully general multi-orbital problem
          is broader than the present paper, we isolate the piece that Part~II can use directly: the decoupled-pocket baseline in which each FS sheet contributes
          additively and deviations can be read as signatures of inter-band structure.
    537 +		The last extension changes not the control parameters but the Hilbert-space structure. Because the fully general multi-orbital problem is broade
         r than the present paper, we isolate the piece that the measurement analysis can use directly: the decoupled-pocket baseline in which each FS sheet cont
         ributes additively and deviations can be read as signatures of inter-band structure.
    538  	
        вӢ®
    553  		
    554 -		Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} is exact for orbital-diagonal pairing. For orbitally mixed pairing it becomes a baseline rather than a theorem:
          deviations diagnose the inter-band structure. The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of the orbital-diagonal ass
         umption; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbital derivation is therefore required
         before importing the simplex interpretation of Part~II, or the normalized site/pocket bounds used there, to genuine inter-band pairing.
    554 +		Equation~\eqref{eq:multiband-id} is exact for orbital-diagonal pairing. For orbitally mixed pairing it becomes a baseline rather than a theorem:
          deviations diagnose the inter-band structure. The Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} itself is independent of the orbital-diagonal ass
         umption; it is the specific anisotropic identity that requires the decoupled-pocket setting. A dedicated multi-orbital derivation is therefore required
         before importing the simplex interpretation of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}, or the normalized site/pocket bounds used later, to genuine inter-band pairing.
    555

    569 -		Equations~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-perp}--\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-par} define a one-parameter family traced by $w_{\parallel}$. As $w_{\parallel}$ increase
         s from $0$ (all weight on the isotropic 3D band) to $1$ (all weight on the quasi-1D bands), the predicted point $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})$ moves from t
         he barycenter $(\tfrac{1}{3},\tfrac{1}{3},\tfrac{1}{3})$ to the vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the simplex of Part~II (Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex}). This one-parameter
         curve is the explicit baseline tested later by Protocol~F and by the worked example of Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}.
    570 -	
    571 -	\section*{Part II: From Identities to Measurement}
    572 -	
    573 -		Part~I produced four kinds of input: a scalar bound, a tensor geometry, controlled departures from the ideal strong-locking limit, and structura
         l extensions to dynamics and multiple pockets. Part~II uses them in the same order an experiment would. First, the strong-locking identity Eq.~\eqref{eq
         :unified}, $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is read directly as a map from the measurable Knight-shift tensor to the projection t
         ensor $\Pi$; this is the ellipsoid theorem of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}. Next, Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the remaining ingredients as successive
         layers of inference: start from the trace bound when only limited data exist, upgrade to the full ellipsoid when rotation data are available, then add f
         inite field, $1/T_1$, site resolution, and pocket decomposition only when needed. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} finally applies that manual to K$_{2}$Cr$_{
         3}$As$_{3}$ in the same order.
    569 +		Equations~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-perp}--\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-par} define a one-parameter family traced by $w_{\parallel}$. As $w_{\parallel}$ increase
         s from $0$ (all weight on the isotropic 3D band) to $1$ (all weight on the quasi-1D bands), the predicted point $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})$ moves from t
         he barycenter $(\tfrac{1}{3},\tfrac{1}{3},\tfrac{1}{3})$ to the vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the simplex in Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex}. This one-parameter curve is t
         he explicit baseline tested later by Protocol~F and by the worked example of Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}.
    570 +
    571 +		The preceding theory sections produced four kinds of input: a scalar bound, a tensor geometry, controlled departures from the ideal strong-locki
         ng limit, and structural extensions to dynamics and multiple pockets. The remaining sections use them in the same order an experiment would. First, the
         strong-locking identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified}, $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)/\chi_N=\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}$, is read directly as a map from the measurable Kn
         ight-shift tensor to the projection tensor $\Pi$; this is the ellipsoid theorem of Sec.~\ref{sec:ellipsoid}. Next, Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} packages the
          remaining ingredients as successive layers of inference: start from the trace bound when only limited data exist, upgrade to the full ellipsoid when ro
         tation data are available, then add finite field, $1/T_1$, site resolution, and pocket decomposition only when needed. Section~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} finall
         y applies that manual to K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ in the same order.
    572  	
        вӢ®
    575  	
    578 -	This section is the conceptual core of Part~II. It turns Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified} into a directly measurable geometric object and then classifies the
         allowed shapes before the protocol section operationalizes the construction.
    576 +	This section is the conceptual core of the measurement side of the paper. It turns Eq.~\eqref{eq:unified} into a directly measurable geometric objec
         t and then classifies the allowed shapes before the protocol section operationalizes the construction.

    615  	\end{equation}
    616 +	This simplex classification is a statement about the locking tensor, not by itself about the parity label. As Sec.~\ref{sec:paritymix-consequences}
         shows, a fully gapped helicity-diagonal parity-mixed state lands on the same $T=0$ ellipsoid as the corresponding pure locking texture; distinguishing p
         arity admixture then requires finite-$T$ information or other probes.

    754  		\end{enumerate}
    755 -	A violation $R>2/3$ signals breakdown of at least one assumption (multi-orbital physics, non-unitary triplet, first-order transition, mixed-parity p
         airing, FFLO, PDW, or strong correlations beyond mean field).
    755 +	A violation $R>2/3$ signals breakdown of at least one assumption (multi-orbital physics, non-unitary triplet, first-order transition, mixed-parity p
         airing outside the helicity-diagonal fully gapped limit, FFLO, PDW, or strong correlations beyond mean field).
    756  	
        вӢ®
    815  		\item Compare across nuclei. Agreement to $\lesssim 5\%$ in every component (typical $T\to 0$ extrapolation resolution) is consistent with the s
         ingle-band unitary BdG framework.
    816 -		\item A systematic discrepancy at the $10$--$20\%$ level indicates a subleading correction: inter-band parity mixing~\cite{Frigeri2004,bauer2012
         non,Samokhin2007}, a non-unitary triplet component with small $\mathbf{m}\propto i\mathbf{d}\times\mathbf{d}^{*}$, or a site-specific orbital contributi
         on not captured by the common geometric kernel.
    816 +		\item A systematic discrepancy at the $10$--$20\%$ level indicates a subleading correction: inter-band structure or non-helicity-diagonal parity
          mixing~\cite{Frigeri2004,bauer2012non,Samokhin2007}, a non-unitary triplet component with small $\mathbf{m}\propto i\mathbf{d}\times\mathbf{d}^{*}$, or
          a site-specific orbital contribution not captured by the common geometric kernel.
    817  		\item A discrepancy $\gtrsim 50\%$ in any component is quantitative evidence that the single-band unitary BdG framework does not apply and a mul
         ti-orbital or multi-$\mathbf{d}$-vector extension is required.
        вӢ®
    835  	
    836 -		With the manual in place, we now close the loop on a concrete material. We apply the protocols of Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} to the $^{75}$As Knig
         ht-shift and $1/T_{1}$ data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021} on single-crystal K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ in the same order as the logic developed
         above: Protocol~B fixes the ellipsoid, Protocol~F tests the decoupled-pocket baseline, and Protocol~D uses the relaxation data to diagnose the missing d
         ynamics. Three findings follow: (a) the observed ellipsoid sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the simplex; (b) the band-resolved SOC-texture b
         aseline Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} disagrees with the observation by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Knight-shift units, favoring a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vecto
         r mechanism within the orbital-diagonal setting; (c) the observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT b
         udget and diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation.
    836 +		With the manual in place, we now close the loop on a concrete material. We apply the protocols of Sec.~\ref{sec:protocols} to the $^{75}$As Knig
         ht-shift and $1/T_{1}$ data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021} on single-crystal K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$ in the same order as the logic developed
         above: Protocol~B fixes the ellipsoid, Protocol~F tests the decoupled-pocket baseline, and Protocol~D uses the relaxation data to diagnose the missing d
         ynamics. Three findings follow: (a) the observed ellipsoid sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of the simplex and therefore fixes a $\hat c$-axis
         locking geometry; (b) the band-resolved SOC-texture baseline Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} disagrees with the observation by $\sim 0.5$ in normalized Kni
         ght-shift units, favoring a common $\hat c$-axis locking interpretation within the orbital-diagonal setting; (c) the observed suppression of $1/T_{1}\pa
         rallel\hat c$ cannot come from the $\mathbf{q}=0$ spin-FGT budget and diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation.
    837

    850  		\end{equation}
    851 -			The observed point sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} within experimental uncertainty, and $\sum\pi_{i}=1\p
         m O(\epsilon)$ saturates the universal constraint Eq.~\eqref{eq:trace-from-ellipsoid}. At the protocol level, this is the complete output of Protocol~B:
          the measured ellipsoid fixes the effective locking texture to the $\hat c$ vertex of the simplex. Within the canonical single-band catalogue of Table~\
         ref{tab:ellipsoids}, this matches the \emph{OSP unitary spin-triplet with $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$} limit, or equivalently the quasi-1D $\hat n\paral
         lel\hat c$ vertex. The main experimental statement therefore precedes any multiband modeling: the Knight-shift tensor itself already selects the $\hat c
         $ vertex.
    851 +			The observed point sits at the oblate-axial vertex $(0,0,1)$ of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex} within experimental uncertainty, and $\sum\pi_{i}=1\p
         m O(\epsilon)$ saturates the universal constraint Eq.~\eqref{eq:trace-from-ellipsoid}. At the protocol level, this is the complete output of Protocol~B:
          the measured ellipsoid fixes the effective locking texture to the $\hat c$ vertex of the simplex. Within the canonical pure-state catalogue of Table~\r
         ef{tab:ellipsoids}, this coincides with the \emph{OSP unitary spin-triplet with $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$} limit, or equivalently the quasi-1D $\hat n
         \parallel\hat c$ vertex. In a noncentrosymmetric system it is also compatible with a fully gapped helicity-diagonal parity-mixed state whose locking geo
         metry is aligned with $\hat c$, as discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:paritymix-consequences}. The main experimental statement therefore concerns the locking ge
         ometry first; the parity content requires finite-$T$ or microscopic input.
    852  		
        вӢ®
    867  	\end{itemize}
    868 -	The Knight-shift ellipsoid alone does not distinguish (a) from (b). DFT calculations~\cite{Jiang15,Wu15}, however, indicate an approximately isotrop
         ic Rashba SOC on the $\gamma$ band, which contradicts (b); the tension captured in Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pred-SOC} makes this contradiction quantitativ
         e. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband}, mechanism (a) is the simplest remaining possibility, and $\hat d=\hat c$ rigidly on
          all pockets yields Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi} exactly. This is consistent with the symmetry analysis of Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} (Table~1 there): the $E
         '$ states $(p_{x}\pm ip_{y})\hat z$ have $\hat d\parallel\hat c$ on all bands.
    868 +	The Knight-shift ellipsoid alone does not distinguish (a) from (b), and a helicity-diagonal parity-mixed state with the same $\hat c$-axis locking g
         eometry would be equally invisible at the level of Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi}. DFT calculations~\cite{Jiang15,Wu15}, however, indicate an approximately
         isotropic Rashba SOC on the $\gamma$ band, which contradicts (b); the tension captured in Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pred-SOC} makes this contradiction quan
         titative. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline of Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband}, mechanism (a) is the simplest remaining possibility, and $\hat d=\hat c$ rig
         idly on all pockets yields Eq.~\eqref{eq:K2Cr3As3-pi} exactly. This is consistent with the symmetry analysis of Ref.~\cite{Triplet2021} (Table~1 there):
          the $E'$ states $(p_{x}\pm ip_{y})\hat z$ have $\hat d\parallel\hat c$ on all bands. A singlet admixture that remains helicity-diagonal would not chang
         e this $T=0$ conclusion.
    869  	
        вӢ®
    886  			\item Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c$ vertex of Fig.~\ref{fig:simplex}, saturating the trace bound.
    887 -			\item Band-resolved SOC-texture prediction Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} with $w_{\gamma}=0.75$ off by $\sim 0.5$ in Knight-shift units; with
         in the orbital-diagonal baseline, the data favor a \emph{rigid common $\mathbf{d}\parallel\hat c$ on all three bands}.
    887 +			\item Band-resolved SOC-texture prediction Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} with $w_{\gamma}=0.75$ off by $\sim 0.5$ in Knight-shift units; with
         in the orbital-diagonal baseline, the data favor a \emph{rigid common $\hat c$-axis locking vector on all three bands}, with common $\mathbf{d}\parallel
         \hat c$ as the simplest realization.
    888  			\item Suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ cannot come from $\mathbf{q}=0$ budget; diagnosed as finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation g
         ap formation.
    889  		\end{enumerate}
    890 -		We distinguish two levels of conclusion. \emph{Robust experimental statement}: the Knight-shift tensor sits at the $\hat c$ vertex, and any acce
         ptable microscopic theory must reproduce that near-vertex ellipsoid. \emph{Baseline theoretical inference}: within the orbital-diagonal framework of Eq.
         ~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred}, the data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline. A definitive mic
         roscopic selection in the presence of strong inter-band pairing requires a multi-orbital BdG analysis---the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-su
         m-rule} survives that extension unchanged, but the specific anisotropic identities and the pocket/site bounds used in Part~II require rederivation.
    890 +		We distinguish two levels of conclusion. \emph{Robust experimental statement}: the Knight-shift tensor sits at the $\hat c$ vertex, and any acce
         ptable microscopic theory must reproduce that near-vertex ellipsoid. \emph{Baseline theoretical inference}: within the orbital-diagonal framework of Eq.
         ~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred}, the data favor a common-$\hat c$ locking interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline; the pure OSP limit
         with a common $\mathbf{d}$ vector is the simplest realization, but a helicity-diagonal parity admixture is not excluded by the $T=0$ ellipsoid alone. A
         definitive microscopic selection in the presence of strong inter-band pairing requires a multi-orbital BdG analysis---the Plancherel identity Eq.~\eqref
         {eq:general-sum-rule} survives that extension unchanged, but the specific anisotropic identities and the pocket/site bounds used later require rederivat
         ion.
    891  	
    892 -	A targeted follow-up is an As1-vs-As2 site-resolved test (Protocol~E): under the common-$\mathbf{d}$-vector interpretation, the normalized projectio
         ns must agree at the $\lesssim 5\%$ level. A systematic discrepancy at the $10$--$20\%$ level would signal inter-band parity mixing or a non-unitary tri
         plet component; a discrepancy $\gtrsim 50\%$ would indicate failure of the single-band unitary BdG framework and require the multi-orbital extension not
         ed above.
    892 +	A targeted follow-up is an As1-vs-As2 site-resolved test (Protocol~E): under a common-$\hat c$ locking interpretation, the normalized projections mu
         st agree at the $\lesssim 5\%$ level. A systematic discrepancy at the $10$--$20\%$ level would signal inter-band structure, strong non-helicity-diagonal
          parity mixing, or a non-unitary triplet component; a discrepancy $\gtrsim 50\%$ would indicate failure of the single-band unitary BdG framework and req
         uire the multi-orbital extension noted above.
    893  	
        вӢ®
    896  	
    897 -		The paper was organized as a progression from one exact identity to experimentally usable diagnostics. Part~I began with the Plancherel identity
          Eq.~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule} and extracted two static $T=0$ consequences in increasing generality: the isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0
         )\le 2\chi_{N}$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:isoT0}) and the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\n
         u}]$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}). These provide, respectively, the lightest scalar consistency test and the full geometric kernel that Part~II reads out ex
         perimentally.
    898 -		
    899 -		Part~I then treated controlled departures and extensions. Section~\ref{sec:intermediate} relaxed the infinite-locking assumption at $\mathbf{H}=
         0$ for $s$-wave pairing and produced the closed-form interpolation $F_{s}(\lambda)$, which contracts the strong-locking simplex radially. Section~\ref{s
         ec:finiteH} restored finite Zeeman field within strong locking and showed quadratic stability of the zero-field ellipsoid. Section~\ref{sec:spinFGT} sup
         plied the dynamical spin-FGT counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and Sec.~\ref{sec:multiband} isolated the decoupled-pocket multiband baseline used later
          as an experimental comparison point.
    897 +		The paper was organized as a progression from one exact identity to experimentally usable diagnostics. Starting from the Plancherel identity Eq.
         ~\eqref{eq:general-sum-rule}, we extracted two static $T=0$ consequences in increasing generality: the isotropic-FS trace bound $\mathrm{Tr}\,\chi(0)\le
          2\chi_{N}$ (Sec.~\ref{sec:isoT0}) and the FS-shape-independent strong-locking tensor identity $\chi_{\mu\nu}(0)=\chi_{N}[\delta_{\mu\nu}-\Pi_{\mu\nu}]$
          (Sec.~\ref{sec:anisoT0}). We then showed how parity-mixed singlet-triplet states fit into the same framework: in the fully gapped helicity-diagonal lim
         it, the $T=0$ projector identity and simplex point are unchanged, while the parity admixture appears primarily in the finite-$T$ helicity-resolved recov
         ery. The later theory sections treated intermediate SOC, finite Zeeman field, the dynamical spin-FGT counterpart relevant to $1/T_{1}$, and the decouple
         d-pocket multiband baseline.
    898  		
    901 -		Part~II then used those outputs in measurement order. Section~\ref{sec:ellipsoid} stated the ellipsoid theorem and pairing simplex; Sec.~\ref{se
         c:protocols} organized six protocols from the lightest scalar check to the multiband baseline test; Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3} applied that manual to the $
         ^{75}$As data of Yang \emph{et al.}~\cite{Triplet2021}. The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ fixes the Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c
         $ vertex and saturates the trace bound. Within the orbital-diagonal baseline, the decoupled-pocket SOC-texture prediction misses the data by $\sim 0.5$,
          so the data favor a common-$\mathbf{d}$ interpretation over the independent-pocket SOC-texture baseline, while the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat
          c$ diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation. The framework therefore unifies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric
          principle and makes explicit which parts of the inference are robust experimentally and which remain baseline-theoretical pending a genuine multi-orbit
         al extension.
    899 +		The measurement sections then turned those outputs into the ellipsoid theorem, six protocols, and the worked example of K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$.
          The observed $(\pi_{a},\pi_{b},\pi_{c})=(0,0,1)$ fixes the Knight-shift ellipsoid at the $\hat c$ vertex and saturates the trace bound. Within the orbi
         tal-diagonal baseline, the decoupled-pocket SOC-texture prediction misses the data by $\sim 0.5$, so the data favor a common $\hat c$-axis locking geome
         try, with a common-$\mathbf{d}$ interpretation as the simplest realization and helicity-diagonal parity admixture not excluded by the $T=0$ ellipsoid al
         one; meanwhile the suppression of $1/T_{1}\parallel\hat c$ diagnoses finite-$\mathbf{q}$ FM spin-fluctuation gap formation. The framework therefore unif
         ies Knight shift and $1/T_{1}$ under a single geometric principle and makes explicit which parts of the inference are robust experimentally and which re
         main baseline-theoretical pending a genuine multi-orbital extension.

вҖў Edited SumRule-v14.tex (+2 -2)
    320  	\end{equation}
    321 -	where $\Delta_{\perp}(\mathbf{k})\propto\mathbf{d}_{\perp}(\mathbf{k})$ mixes the two helicity sheets. The geometrically simplest regime is therefor
         e the \emph{helicity-diagonal} limit $\Delta_{\perp}=0$, realized exactly when $\mathbf{d}\parallel\mathbf{g}$ and approximately whenever the antisymmet
         ric SOC splitting is large compared with transverse inter-helicity pairing. In that limit, parity mixing changes the gap amplitudes on the $\pm$ sheets
         but not the underlying locking texture $\hat n_{\mathbf{k}}=\hat g_{\mathbf{k}}$.
    321 +	where $\Delta_{\perp}(\mathbf{k})$ is set by the transverse component $\mathbf{d}_{\perp}(\mathbf{k})$ and mixes the two helicity sheets. The geomet
         rically simplest regime is therefore the \emph{helicity-diagonal} limit $\Delta_{\perp}=0$, realized exactly when $\mathbf{d}\parallel\mathbf{g}$ and ap
         proximately whenever the antisymmetric SOC splitting is large compared with transverse inter-helicity pairing. In that limit, parity mixing changes the
         gap amplitudes on the $\pm$ sheets but not the underlying locking texture $\hat n_{\mathbf{k}}=\hat g_{\mathbf{k}}$.
    322
        вӢ®
    828  			\item Apply pressure or composition tuning that shifts $w_{\parallel}$; track $\chi_{zz}(0)/\chi_{N}$ along the one-parameter family Eq.~\eq
         ref{eq:a2cr3as3-par}.
    829 -			\item Agreement of the observed trajectory with Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} tests the band-resolved SOC-texture hypothesis of Sec.~\ref{sec
         :multiband-A2Cr3As3}. Disagreement (as in K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}) favors a different mechanism---most simply, a common $\mathb
         f{d}$-vector across all bands within the orbital-diagonal baseline.
    829 +			\item Agreement of the observed trajectory with Eq.~\eqref{eq:a2cr3as3-pred} tests the band-resolved SOC-texture hypothesis of Sec.~\ref{sec
         :multiband-A2Cr3As3}. Disagreement (as in K$_{2}$Cr$_{3}$As$_{3}$, Sec.~\ref{sec:K2Cr3As3}) favors a different mechanism---most simply, a common locking
          texture across all bands, with a common $\mathbf{d}$ vector as the simplest orbital-diagonal realization.
    830  		\end{enumerate}0;в ҷ X-Sum-Rule

  I removed the visible part-style structure from the main text, rewrote the abstract/introduction/transitions/summary so the paper no longer reads as Part I /
  Part II, and added two new theory sections after the strong-locking section: Parity-mixed pairing I: helicity-basis structure and Parity-mixed pairing II: what0;в ё X-Sum-Rule

  survives and what changes. The new material treats parity mixture as singlet-triplet mixing in the helicity basis, shows that the fully gapped helicity-
  diagonal limit keeps the same (T=0) projector/ellipsoid, and shifts the main parity diagnostic to finite-(T) recovery.0;в ј X-Sum-Rule

  Older manuscript generations SumRule-v8* through SumRule-v13* and their build artifacts were moved into backupTeX. Unused figure outputs were moved into
  backup/unused-figures. In particular, I kept only the figure actually referenced by the current manuscript, figures/fig3_t0_validation.pdf, in figures/.0;в № X-Sum-Rule
0;в ё X-Sum-Rule


  I also removed the current v14 auxiliary clutter from the top level (.aux, .blg, .fdb_latexmk, .fls, .log, .toc). The active top-level manuscript set is now
  essentially SumRule-v14.tex, SumRule-v14.pdf, SumRule-v14.bbl, SumRule-v14Notes.bib, mag-soc.bib, and the validation/check scripts.

  If you want, I can do one more pass and archive the validation/check scripts too, leaving only the manuscript, bibliography, and active figure.0;X-Sum-Rule

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖвҖўWorking(40s вҖў esc to interrupt) вҖә Use /skills to list available skills gpt-5.4 xhigh В· /mnt/c/Usr/yizhou/TeX/publish/X-Sum-RuleMM

вҖў In the appendix, вҖңRMSвҖқ means root-mean-square deviation of the extracted data points from the theoretical curve. See SumRule-v14.tex:913.

  Here it means
  [
  \mathrm{RMS}

  \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \left(\Delta_i\right)^2},
  \qquad
  \Delta_i вҖә Use /skills to list available skills gpt-5.4 xhigh В· /mnt/c/Usr/yizhou/TeX/publish/X-Sum-Rule

  \left[\frac{\chi(0)}{\chi_N}\right]^{\rm digitized}_i

  \left[\frac{\chi(0)}{\chi_N}\right]^{\rm theory}_i.
  ]
0;в § X-Sum-Rule

  So:

  - max deviation = 1.5Г—10^{-3} is the worst mismatch among the extracted points.
  - RMS = 7.7Г—10^{-4} is the typical mismatch size overall.

  In this appendix, it is the RMS over the extracted (T=0) intercepts used for the Fig. 3 cross-check.0;в Ү X-Sum-Rule
0;в Ҹ X-Sum-Rule
0;в Ӣ X-Sum-Rule


  If you want, I can change the text to say вҖңroot-mean-square deviationвҖ0;X-Sum-Rule

    986 -			\caption{Schematic: $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$ in the normal state (blue) and the SC state (red). Spectral weight of magnit
         ude $\chi_{N}\pi_{\mu}$ is transferred from the static peak at $\omega\to 0$ into the pair-breaking continuum above $2\Delta_{\min}$, conserving the Kra
         mers--Kronig integral Eq.~\eqref{eq:spinFGT}. The Van Vleck bump at the interband threshold is essentially unaffected by pairing at leading order in $\D
         elta/|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|$.}
    987 -			\label{fig:spinFGT-spectral}
    988 -		\end{figure}

    971 +					\draw[thick,blue] (0,0) -- (0.02,2.6);
    972 +					\draw[thick,blue] (0.02,2.6) -- (0.2,0.2);
    973 +					\draw[thick,blue] (0.2,0.2) -- (4,0.2) .. controls (4.5,0.5) and (5,0.5) .. (5.5,0.2) -- (7,0.2);
    974 +					\node[blue] at (1,2.3) {\footnotesize normal};
    975 +					\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0,0) -- (0.02,1.4);
    976 +					\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0.02,1.4) -- (0.2,0.1);

    978 +					\draw[thick,red,dashed] (2.5,0.1) .. controls (3,0.8) and (3.5,0.9) .. (4.5,0.6) .. controls (5.2,0.4) and (6,0.3) .. (7,0.25);
    979 +					\node[red] at (4.5,1.1) {\footnotesize SC};
    980 +					\draw[dashed,gray] (2.5,0) -- (2.5,1);
    981 +					\node[below] at (2.5,-0.05) {\footnotesize $2\Delta_{\min}$};

    986 +			\refstepcounter{figure}\label{fig:spinFGT-spectral}
    987 +			\noindent\textbf{FIG. \thefigure.} Schematic: $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$ in the normal state (blue) and the SC state (red).
          Spectral weight of magnitude $\chi_{N}\pi_{\mu}$ is transferred from the static peak at $\omega\to 0$ into the pair-breaking continuum above $2\Delta_{
         \min}$, conserving the Kramers--Kronig integral Eq.~\eqref{eq:spinFGT}. The Van Vleck bump at the interband threshold is essentially unaffected by pairi
         ng at leading order in $\Delta/|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|$.

MM

вҖў Updated SumRule-v15.tex and regenerated SumRule-v15.pdf. In the appendix, вҖңRMSвҖқ is now written out as вҖңroot-mean-square (RMS) deviationвҖқ at SumRule-
  v15.tex:951. I also tightened the key-results table typography and converted the appendix figures to inline figure blocks so the end-of-document float issue is
  gone.

  Final pass check: latexmk completes successfully, bibliography and cross-references resolve, and the previous stuck-float warning is removed. The remaining
  compile messages are routine REVTeX underfull box warnings only.

    986 -			\refstepcounter{figure}\label{fig:spinFGT-spectral}
    987 -			\noindent\textbf{FIG. \thefigure.} Schematic: $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$ in the normal state (blue) and the SC state (red).
          Spectral weight of magnitude $\chi_{N}\pi_{\mu}$ is transferred from the static peak at $\omega\to 0$ into the pair-breaking continuum above $2\Delta_{
         \min}$, conserving the Kramers--Kronig integral Eq.~\eqref{eq:spinFGT}. The Van Vleck bump at the interband threshold is essentially unaffected by pairi
         ng at leading order in $\Delta/|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|$.
    988 -			\par
    967 +				\begin{figure}[tb]
    968 +					\centering
    969 +					\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.9]
    970 +						\draw[->] (0,0) -- (7,0) node[right] {$\omega$};
    971 +						\draw[->] (0,0) -- (0,3) node[above] {$\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$};
    972 +						\draw[thick,blue] (0,0) -- (0.02,2.6);
    973 +						\draw[thick,blue] (0.02,2.6) -- (0.2,0.2);
    974 +						\draw[thick,blue] (0.2,0.2) -- (4,0.2) .. controls (4.5,0.5) and (5,0.5) .. (5.5,0.2) -- (7,0.2);
    975 +						\node[blue] at (1,2.3) {\footnotesize normal};
    976 +						\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0,0) -- (0.02,1.4);
    977 +						\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0.02,1.4) -- (0.2,0.1);
    978 +						\draw[thick,red,dashed] (0.2,0.1) -- (2.5,0.1);
    979 +						\draw[thick,red,dashed] (2.5,0.1) .. controls (3,0.8) and (3.5,0.9) .. (4.5,0.6) .. controls (5.2,0.4) and (6,0.3) .. (7,0.25);
    980 +						\node[red] at (4.5,1.1) {\footnotesize SC};
    981 +						\draw[dashed,gray] (2.5,0) -- (2.5,1);
    982 +						\node[below] at (2.5,-0.05) {\footnotesize $2\Delta_{\min}$};
    983 +						\draw[<->, thick, black] (-0.4,1.4) -- (-0.4,2.6);
    984 +						\node[left] at (-0.4,2.0) {\footnotesize $\chi_N\pi_\mu$};
    985 +					\end{tikzpicture}
    986 +					\caption{Schematic: $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\mu\mu}(\omega)/\omega$ in the normal state (blue) and the SC state (red). Spectral weight o
         f magnitude $\chi_{N}\pi_{\mu}$ is transferred from the static peak at $\omega\to 0$ into the pair-breaking continuum above $2\Delta_{\min}$, conserving
          the Kramers--Kronig integral Eq.~\eqref{eq:spinFGT}. The Van Vleck bump at the interband threshold is essentially unaffected by pairing at leading orde
         r in $\Delta/|\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{k}}|$.}
    987 +					\label{fig:spinFGT-spectral}
    988 +				\end{figure}
